01-sit-stay-preach.title: Sit. Stay. Preach.
01-sit-stay-preach.subtitle: Twelve Lessons From Dogs About Loving God

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord.title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

excerpts.text: We’ve never met someone in ministry who didn’t need a good dog. Some need one to walk with in the early mornings before the world gets demanding. Some need one to listen, really listen, without interrupting, correcting, or offering ministry advice. Some just need the reminder that joy doesn’t come from metrics, but from a wagging tail and someone who is glad that you came home. Over the years, we’ve had many dogs. Some were loyal. Some were stubborn. One barked at shadows for reasons known only to God. But each one, in their own doggish way, taught us something we didn’t expect to learn: patience, presence, joy, grief, and what it means to be faithful when no one’s watching.

This isn’t a how-to book on church growth. This won’t fix your elders, your deacons, or your sound system. It’s not even a book of polished answers. It’s more like a box of dog biscuits: some soft, some crunchy, and a few that might need chewing on before the goodness comes through. Sit. Stay. Preach. is about noticing. Noticing the things we often overlook in ministry and in life. It’s about finding God in the everyday moments. About knowing when to speak and when to stay silent. About remembering that even the Good Shepherd lets His sheep rest beside still waters. And maybe it’s okay if the pastor lies down too.

We are not pastors. We are one deacon and one lay person trying to love God well. These reflections are for anyone walking quietly, faithfully, still learning how to stay close to the Shepherd. In a world that spans many cultures, languages, and traditions, we’ve discovered that dogs speak a universal language of love, and God’s grace translates perfectly into every heart.

Mola had mastered something Min-jung had lost in all her busyness: the art of staying close. Min-jung remembered Jesus’s words: “Remain in me, and I will remain in you.” Not “work for me” or “impress me with your programs.” Remain. Stay close. Be near. She thought about Mary and Martha in the Bible. Martha had been busy serving Jesus, worried about getting everything perfect. But Mary had chosen to sit at Jesus’s feet, simply being with Him. And Jesus had gently told Martha that Mary had chosen the better way.

Min-jung realized she had been living like Martha for years. Always busy, always worried about the next event or meeting or problem to solve. She had been so focused on serving Jesus that she had forgotten to simply be with Him. There’s a reason Scripture describes God as our shelter, our refuge, our hiding place. And there’s a reason Jesus calls us to remain in Him. We dash in and out of God’s presence like we’re picking up dry cleaning. We offer quick prayers between meetings and squeeze in hurried devotions before bed. But dwelling isn’t a drive-thru. It’s sitting down in the shade and staying there.

And somehow, in that quiet space, the lesson she had been wrestling with began to write itself. Not because she was working harder, but because she had finally stopped working and started trusting. Mola had taught her something that church leaders often forget: sometimes the most spiritual thing we can do is sit down, stay close to God, and let His presence work in us. We don’t have to carry the weight of ministry on our shoulders. We don’t have to have all the answers or solve every problem. We just need to remain close to the One who does.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Trust God's Timing
excerpts.text: Neil was amazed. If they had rushed ahead with their own program months earlier, they would have missed this opportunity to partner with other churches and have a much greater impact. “How did you know to wait?” Neil asked. Pastor Moonsamy explained, “I’ve learned that God’s timing is always perfect, even when it doesn’t make sense to us. When we wait for His signal, we receive His best plans. Scripture doesn’t say those who hurry will renew their strength. It says those who wait on the Lord will renew their strength.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Trust God's Timing
excerpts.text: Neil thought about Asante again. The dog didn’t wait because he didn’t want the food. He waited because he trusted his master more than he trusted his own hunger. Over the following months, Neil began to approach ministry differently. Instead of immediately jumping into action when he saw a need, he started by praying and seeking God’s timing. He found that when ministries launched at God’s timing rather than his own, they were more successful and brought more glory to God.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Trust God's Timing
excerpts.text: When the homeless shelter ministry finally began, it was better than anything Neil could have planned on his own. The three churches working together had more volunteers, more resources, and more wisdom than any one church could have provided. Neil learned that waiting on God isn’t wasted time. It’s worship. It’s a way of saying, “I trust You more than I trust my own desire to serve.” And when God finally gives the signal to move forward, the results are always worth the wait.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About God's Faithful Love
excerpts.text: Pastor Kim thought about God’s love. God doesn’t love His people because they’re impressive or talented or useful in ministry programs. He loves them because He chooses to love them. His love isn’t based on what they accomplish. It’s based on His character. Just like Charlie waiting at the door, God’s love is patient and constant. He doesn’t give up on His people when they’re late to church or struggling with faith. He doesn’t stop loving them when they mess up or fail to serve. He keeps showing up, keeps waiting, keeps loving them with the kind of faithful heart that never lets go.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About God's Faithful Love
excerpts.text: Some kinds of love are worth waiting for. And Charlie’s faithfulness changed Pastor Kim. Charlie found a new home that day. But more importantly, Pastor Kim found a new understanding of how much God loves His people. The kind of love that waits at the door, no matter how long it takes for them to come home. Pastor Kim began to see his congregation differently. Instead of focusing on who was missing, he started noticing who was faithfully showing up. Instead of worrying about declining numbers, he started celebrating the steady hearts like Mr. Henderson who came quietly to pray. Instead of measuring success by programs and activities, he started measuring it by faithfulness and love.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About God's Faithful Love
excerpts.text: And every morning when Pastor Kim saw Charlie in their backyard, he was reminded that the best ministry isn’t always the most visible. Sometimes it’s just showing up, staying close, and trusting that God sees every act of quiet faithfulness.

What amazed Elena most was how Bella could rest no matter what was happening around her. A garbage truck rumbled down the street, and Bella barely lifted her head. The neighbor’s lawnmower started up, but Bella stayed peaceful. A squirrel ran across the fence just feet from her nose, but Bella simply opened one eye, looked, and went back to resting. Elena realized that Bella somehow knew she was safe. The yard was secure. Her food bowl was full. Elena was watching over her. That was enough for Bella to rest completely.

As she watched Bella sleep so peacefully, Elena began to think about her own relationship with rest. Elena realized she had been serving at the church for years, always busy, always working on something. She felt guilty whenever she wasn’t being productive, as if resting meant she wasn’t doing her part. Watching Bella, Elena realized something important. Bella wasn’t resting because she was lazy or didn’t care. She was resting because she felt completely safe. Her food bowl was full, the yard was secure, and Elena was watching over her. That complete trust allowed Bella to rest peacefully. Elena understood that rest wasn’t laziness - it was trust.

Elena remembered Jesus’s words: “Come to me and I will give you rest.” Not “work harder for me” or “prove your worth to me.” Come and rest. She thought about how Jesus himself had rested. He slept in a boat during a storm while His disciples worried and panicked. That wasn’t because Jesus didn’t care about the danger. It was because He trusted His Father completely. Elena realized she had been carrying burdens that weren’t meant for her shoulders. She worried about every administrative detail as if the church would fall apart without her constant attention. She stressed about events as if their success was her responsibility alone. She had forgotten that the church belonged to God, not to her.

They sat together for almost an hour. Elena felt her shoulders relax for the first time in weeks. Her mind slowly stopped racing through her mental to-do list. And in that quiet space, she remembered why she loved serving at the church in the first place. It wasn’t about the busy work or the endless tasks. It was about participating in God’s work and trusting Him with the results. When Mrs. Rodriguez returned the following Sunday, she found Elena looking more rested than she had in months.

Mrs. Rodriguez nodded knowingly. “Ah, yes. Sometimes we can learn from our furry friends, can’t we? They don’t worry about tomorrow’s problems like we do.” From that week forward, Elena began to approach her church work differently. She still served faithfully and worked hard, but she also built regular times of rest into her schedule. She learned to trust that God could handle the church’s needs even when she took breaks. And every time Elena felt the old anxiety creeping back, she remembered Bella sleeping peacefully in the sunshine, completely trusting that everything important was taken care of. If a dog could rest in such trust, surely she could learn to rest in God’s care too.

Elena discovered that when she rested well, she actually served better. She had more patience with difficult volunteers, more creativity for solving problems, and more joy in her daily tasks. Rest hadn’t made her less effective. It had made her more like the person God designed her to be.

excerpts.lesson_title: Not Every Dog Wins Best in Show
excerpts.text: Jabari realized that he had been measuring his worth by the wrong standards. The world might celebrate the most impressive facilities and the biggest budgets. Social media might highlight the churches with the most beautiful buildings. But God cared more about faithfulness than fame. He cared more about a servant’s heart than a servant’s resources. Over the following months, Jabari began to see his work differently. Instead of focusing on what other churches had that his didn’t, he started noticing the ways his faithful service made a difference. When elderly members mentioned how much they appreciated the clean, comfortable building, he remembered that his work mattered. When children played safely on the playground equipment he maintained, he saw the value of his careful attention to detail.

excerpts.lesson_title: Not Every Dog Wins Best in Show
excerpts.text: Jabari learned that his worth wasn’t measured by comparison to other churches or other facilities managers. It was measured by his faithfulness to the work God had given him. And in God’s eyes, no faithful service is too small or too unnoticed. Just like Rusty had found a family who valued loyalty over appearance, Jabari had found his place in God’s work where faithfulness mattered more than fame.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning When to Speak and When to Stay Quiet
excerpts.text: Ruth realized that by speaking less often, her words had become more valuable. When everything is urgent, nothing feels urgent. When every expense is questioned, the real problems get lost in the noise. Over the following months, Ruth practiced what she had learned from Baxter. She still cared deeply about the church’s finances, but she became more selective about when to voice her concerns. She learned to distinguish between issues that truly needed her expertise and matters that the board could handle without her input.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning When to Speak and When to Stay Quiet
excerpts.text: She found that when she spoke up less frequently, people listened more carefully when she did have something important to say. Board meetings became more collaborative, and Ruth felt like her contributions were more respected and effective. Ruth also learned that silence could be a form of wisdom. Sometimes the most helpful thing she could do was listen to other perspectives, let discussions develop naturally, and only speak when her expertise was truly needed. Just like a good watchdog that saves its bark for real threats, Ruth discovered that saving her voice for important matters made her a more effective treasurer and a better board member.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Ministry Through Presence
excerpts.text: “Buddy doesn’t try to fix anything,” Daniel continued. “He doesn’t tell me it’s all going to be okay or that I should look on the bright side. He just stays with me. That helps more than all the advice.” As James reflected on Daniel’s words, he began to understand something important about ministry. So often, church leaders feel pressure to have the right words, the perfect Scripture verse, or the solution to people’s problems. But maybe the most powerful ministry wasn’t always about what you said or did. Maybe sometimes it was simply about showing up and staying close.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Ministry Through Presence
excerpts.text: When the choir director’s elderly mother was dying, James didn’t try to explain God’s timing or offer comfort through theological reasoning. He just checked in regularly and asked practical questions about what kind of support would be most helpful. James learned that you don’t need a seminary degree to minister to people. You don’t need perfect words or profound insights. Sometimes the most meaningful ministry is simply being willing to enter into someone else’s pain and stay there with them, just like Buddy had done for Tom.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Ministry Through Presence
excerpts.text: Six months after Maya’s death, Daniel stopped by James’s office at the church. “I wanted to thank you,” Daniel said. “For all those times you just sat with me. You didn’t try to make everything better or give me easy answers. You just stayed. That meant more than you know.” James smiled, thinking of Buddy’s faithful presence during those difficult weeks. He had learned something important from that old dog: sometimes the best thing you can offer someone is simply to show up and stay with them.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Ministry Through Presence
excerpts.text: Daniel’s eyes grew a little misty. “You know, Maya and Buddy had such a special bond. During all those months of treatments, he never left her side. And she would talk to him like he was her best friend. I think they understood each other in a way that went beyond words.” James realized that Buddy had taught him one of the most important lessons about church ministry: presence is often more powerful than words, and staying close to people in their pain is sometimes the most Christ-like thing you can do.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Hope That Doesn't Give Up
excerpts.text: Pastor Martinez looked at Gida, who hadn’t moved from his post by the window. The dog’s ears were alert, his eyes bright, and his tail occasionally gave a hopeful wag when a car drove by. Gida didn’t check the time or complain about waiting. He simply believed that David would come home, and he was going to be ready when it happened. “Doesn’t he get tired of waiting?” Pastor Martinez asked. Mrs. Kim smiled softly. “I used to worry about that. I tried to get him to play with toys or go for walks, thinking he needed distractions from his waiting. But I realized something: Gida isn’t suffering while he waits. He’s hopeful.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Hope That Doesn't Give Up
excerpts.text: Over the following weeks, Pastor Martinez found himself thinking about Gida’s faithful waiting. The dog had no control over when David would return. He couldn’t call the military for updates or check deployment schedules online. All Gida could do was wait with hope and trust. Pastor Martinez began to see similarities between Gida’s situation and his own prayers about the church building. He had been treating his waiting like a problem to solve rather than an opportunity to trust. He had grown impatient and discouraged, assuming that delayed answers meant his requests had been denied.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Hope That Doesn't Give Up
excerpts.text: But Gida demonstrated a different kind of waiting; his waiting was one filled with expectation rather than frustration. Gida wasn’t angry that David hadn’t returned yet. He wasn’t checking his watch or giving up hope. He was simply maintaining his faithful vigil, believing that good things were worth waiting for. The next Sunday, Pastor Martinez preached about hope that endures. He talked about how waiting on God isn’t passive resignation, but active faith. It’s believing that God’s timing is perfect even when we can’t see the bigger picture.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Hope That Doesn't Give Up
excerpts.text: “Sometimes we think that if God doesn’t answer our prayers quickly, He’s forgotten about us,” Pastor Martinez told his congregation. “But maybe His delays are part of His perfect plan. Maybe He’s preparing something better than we originally asked for.” A few months later, Pastor Martinez received an unexpected phone call. A local business owner had heard about their church’s needs through a mutual friend. This man wanted to donate a piece of land, which was much larger and better located than anything the church could have afforded to buy. The building they could construct on this land would serve not just their current congregation, but could accommodate future growth and community outreach programs they had only dreamed about.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Hope That Doesn't Give Up
excerpts.text: A few weeks later, Mrs. Kim called Pastor Martinez with excitement in her voice. David was coming home. His deployment had ended earlier than expected, and he would be arriving that weekend. When David finally walked through the front door, Gida’s joy was explosive. His tail wagged so hard his whole body shook. He danced and spun in circles, as if all his patient waiting had finally paid off. Pastor Martinez realized that Gida had taught him something important about faith. Hope isn’t about knowing when prayers will be answered. Hope is about believing they will be answered and staying faithful while you wait.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning About Hope That Doesn't Give Up
excerpts.text: Just like Gida painted welcome signs with his wagging tail, believers can live with expectant hearts, trusting that God’s love never forgets and His timing is always perfect. The church’s new building project began six months later, but Pastor Martinez had already received the greater gift - learning how to wait with hope and trust.

excerpts.lesson_title: The Joy in a Tennis Ball
excerpts.text: But to Sunny, it was pure treasure. He tossed the ball up in the air and caught it. He dropped it and pounced on it like he was hunting. He carried it around the yard like he had just discovered the most wonderful thing in the world. His tail wagged so hard his whole body wiggled with delight. Amira found herself smiling as she watched Sunny’s pure enthusiasm. Here was a dog who could find complete joy in something so simple and worn out. He didn’t need a brand new toy or expensive equipment to be happy. That old tennis ball was enough to make his entire day.

excerpts.lesson_title: The Joy in a Tennis Ball
excerpts.text: Over the next few weeks, Amira continued to watch Sunny during her morning coffee time. Every day, he played with that same ball with the same level of excitement. It never got old for him. It never became boring or disappointing. Each time he saw it, his eyes lit up with genuine delight. Amira began to think about her own attitude toward the church’s music ministry. She had been so focused on what they didn’t have that she had stopped appreciating what they did have. Yes, their equipment was older, but it still worked. Yes, their music stands were mismatched, but they held the music. Yes, the piano needed tuning, but it still made beautiful sounds when the congregation sang together.

excerpts.lesson_title: The Joy in a Tennis Ball
excerpts.text: More importantly, Amira realized she had been missing the real treasure in her work. Every Sunday, she got to help people worship God through music. She got to see elderly members singing hymns they had loved for decades. She got to hear children learning new songs about God’s love. She got to watch the whole congregation lift their voices together in praise. These precious moments weren’t dependent on having perfect equipment or unlimited budgets. They were about hearts connecting with God through simple melodies and familiar words. The next Sunday, Amira approached the service with a different attitude. Instead of worrying about the microphone’s slight feedback or the piano’s imperfect tuning, she focused on the joy of making music together. When the adult choir sang with the kind of heartfelt emotion that only comes from years of faith, Amira felt tears in her eyes. When the children’s choir giggled their way through their special song, Amira couldn’t help but smile.

excerpts.lesson_title: The Joy in a Tennis Ball
excerpts.text: That week, Amira started a gratitude list for her music ministry. Instead of focusing on what was lacking, she wrote down what was working well. The list grew longer than she expected: dedicated choir members who showed up every week, a pianist who volunteered her time faithfully, a sound system that might be old but never failed them, and a congregation that sang with genuine hearts. Amira learned something important from watching Sunny’s daily joy. Happiness doesn’t come from having the newest or best things. It comes from appreciating what you have and finding delight in simple blessings.

excerpts.lesson_title: The Joy in a Tennis Ball
excerpts.text: Just like Sunny could play with the same old tennis ball day after day with fresh enthusiasm, Amira discovered she could lead music ministry with renewed joy, even with the same limited resources. The secret wasn’t in getting better equipment; it was in having a grateful heart that could see the treasure in what was already there. From that point forward, whenever Amira felt discouraged about budget constraints or equipment limitations, she would think of Sunny’s pure delight with his worn-out tennis ball.

This weekly drama is more than dog behavior. Like Penny, we have our own habit of wandering off from the safe boundaries of love. We go after interesting things down roads that lead away from home. We explore places that promise excitement but ultimately leave us unsafe, muddy, and tired. And like Penny, at the end of our adventure we find ourselves standing on the porch, hoping to find forgiveness and acceptance. The truth is that God’s door will always open. He always says, “There you are. I’ve been waiting for you.”

This is how God responds when we find ourselves bitter about our faithful service or resentful that others seem to get grace we think we deserve, not them. He doesn’t criticize our hard work or tell us we shouldn’t care about doing things right. He simply reminds us that it’s all about love from the very beginning. The door is always open not just for the wanderers, but for the workers who have forgotten why they started working in the first place.

When visitors come to the house, other neighborhood dogs might race to the fence, barking to let everyone know and wanting attention. Mola looks from her spot in the sun, and wags her tail, just a bit to say, “I see you.” She seems to understand something that many others don’t: she already has a place, a name, and a family. She doesn’t need to earn what she already has. This behavior becomes even more interesting during stressful situations. When construction noise fills the air, or when storms bring thunder and lightning, Mola doesn’t panic or hide. She simply moves closer to her family members. Her calm presence suggests a deep security, not in her circumstances, but in her relationships.

This pattern reveals something that extends far beyond dog behavior. In church leadership, we often struggle with the names people give us or the names we give ourselves. We get called “successful” or “struggling.” We answer to “popular preacher” or “small church pastor.” We respond to whispers of “not enough” or “too much” or “should be doing better by now.” We can get so focused on the specific labels that we lose sight of the relationship behind them. God doesn’t call us by how well we do our jobs or how well our ministry is going. He doesn’t call us by our failures or our achievements. When He speaks our name, He’s calling us into a relationship: the same relationship that He’s always wanted with us.

The Bible tells us about a man who struggled with names and identity his entire life. Jacob’s name meant “deceiver,” and he lived up to it for years. He manipulated his brother, tricked his father, and spent decades running from the consequences of his choices. But one night, after wrestling with God until dawn, he received a new name: Israel, which means “God fights for you.” The interesting thing is that even after receiving his new name, Jacob was still called by his old name throughout Scripture. But something had changed. The name didn’t define him anymore. What mattered wasn’t what people called him, but who he belonged to and who was fighting for him.

This is the heart of what God wants church leaders to understand. We may answer to many names in ministry; some are encouraging and some are discouraging. People might call us gifted or ordinary, successful or struggling, important or unknown. But underneath all those labels is the one name that truly matters: beloved child of God. When church leaders understand whose voice really counts, they can serve with the same confidence that Mola displays. We don’t need to perform at all, let alone for approval or compete for attention. We don’t have to prove our worth through bigger ministries or better performance. We can simply rest knowing that when God calls our name, He’s inviting us into the most important relationship in this life, and beyond.

During family parties when the house is filled with noise and activity, other dogs might get excited and run around trying to get attention from every visitor. Yuki says hello to the guests with a polite tail wag, then goes back to her usual spot. She’s learned that her job isn’t to entertain; it’s to be there for the one person who matters most to her. As Yuki has grown older, her movements have slowed, but her faithfulness has never changed. Even when her joints are stiff, she still makes the trip from her bed to the living room each evening. Even when she’s tired, she finds the energy to be there for him. Her commitment to showing up has nothing to do with how she feels and everything to do with who she loves.

What’s most amazing is that Yuki shows up simply because that’s what faithful friends do. She doesn’t ask for treats, praise, or attention. Her reward is the relationship itself. This faithful presence has become such a big part of the Chen family routine that they only notice it when it’s missing. On the rare times when Yuki is at the veterinarian or staying with relatives, Mr. Chen finds himself looking down at his feet during his evening reading time. The space feels empty.

Yuki’s approach to faithfulness is beautiful in its simplicity. She can’t understand most problems. Most challenges are too big for her. She just shows up, stays close, and remains available. By doing that, she is saying: “You’re not alone. I’m here. I’m not going anywhere.” After years of this evening routine, it becomes clear that the biggest gift Yuki gives isn’t entertainment or protection from danger; it’s the security of knowing that she cares enough to show up every day, day after day.

For church leaders who often feel overwhelmed by the pressure to do more, be more, and accomplish more, Yuki’s story offers a simple way to be faithful. Sometimes the most important ministry isn’t preaching the perfect sermon or starting the newest program or solving everyone’s problems. Sometimes it’s simply showing up every week and being with God and with His people. Almost all church leaders feel like they’re not doing enough. But sometimes the most powerful ministry does not happen in the spotlight; it happens in quiet moments of being there for God’s people. Showing up for the same people week after week. Being available when someone needs prayer. Staying close to God especially when the work feels ordinary.

The world needs people who are faithful, who are there, and shows up day after day, week after week. The world needs people who understand that sometimes the greatest gift we can offer is simply being there, ready and available, at the feet of the One who loves us most. When we learn this kind of faithfulness, we discover that we don’t have to be impressive to be important. We just have to show up. God sees every quiet act of showing up, and every decision to stay even when it would be easier to leave. He knows the importance of simply being there, and He’s honored by the kind of unshakeable faithfulness that keeps going.

Does today feel ordinary? Does ministry seem less than exciting? Does no one notice the work being done? Then remember Yuki. Sometimes the most important thing we can do is find our place at God’s feet and stay there. And be ready to follow wherever He leads us. The Bible tells us about someone who understood this kind of faithfulness. Even when His disciples fell asleep during His darkest hour, Jesus stayed awake and kept praying. Even when the crowds turned against Him, He kept showing up. Even when following God’s plan led to suffering and death, He stayed exactly where He was supposed to be.

Jesus didn’t promise His followers that following Him would always be exciting or that every day would bring big victories. What He promised was His presence: “I am with you always, even to the end of the world.” That’s the heart of faithfulness.

We didn’t write this book because we had all the answers. Honestly, we wrote it because we needed to remember some things we’d forgotten. About God. About grace. About how following Jesus isn’t about being the best. Following Jesus is about staying close to Him. All the dogs in our stories were far from perfect. Mola wasn’t a perfect dog, either. She didn’t know much. Or maybe she did and just didn’t let on. But she, along with all the others, taught us more than we expected: about loyalty, about resting, about how presence is sometimes the most powerful kind of ministry.

These stories came from watching dogs. They came from diverse neighborhoods where different languages are spoken, different foods are shared, but where the same God is loved and served. We learned that faithfulness has no accent. Love speaks every language. And God’s grace translates perfectly into every culture. We’re not theologians. We’re not pastors. We’re just two people — one deacon and one lay person — who love Jesus and have learned from good dogs in a beautifully diverse world. And if you made it all the way here, maybe you’re someone who’s tired, or hurting, or trying your best to be faithful in a world that often forgets what that word even means.

If that’s you, we hope these stories reminded you that God sees you. He hasn’t forgotten. You’re not too small. You’re not too late. You’re not too far gone. You’re loved. So wherever you are, however you’re walking, and whatever language you pray in, we’ll just say this: Keep going. Stay close to the Shepherd. And when in doubt: sit, stay, and listen for His voice. He speaks to every heart. In every tongue. And sometimes, even through a good dog He places in our path now and then.

And the lessons started lining up. Strangely and beautifully, with the very truths we’d been reading in God’s Word. Jin Kim is walking through stage 4 colon cancer. A season when life slows down whether you want it to or not. When the world turns a little slower, unimportant things go out of focus and certain important things come into focus. You start to notice what really matters. And often, it’s presence: the presence of God. God’s love. God’s grace. Everywhere, especially in unexpected places.

Dogs seem to know some things instinctively. Things God, in His mercy and grace, keeps showing us through them. We’re just two ordinary believers. One deacon. One layperson. Trying to love God well. Trying to walk faithfully. Still learning what it means to stay close to the Lord. And if you are too, this book is for you. He is with you. Always. 4

Good dogs know how to sit. They don’t rush things. They don’t panic; well most don’t. They just wait. And when they’re told to sit, they usually do. Not always right away. Sometimes they squirm a little. But in the end, they stay. Oddly enough, that, for a lot of us human beings, is really hard to do. When life gets hard, we want to fix it. We want to do something about it, anything, to make it better. But maybe sometimes, what God wants from us is something quite different, much smaller.

He looked up, smiled, and answered, “Waiting on the Lord. He’s almost never early. But He’s never late either.” He wasn’t doing nothing. He was waiting, with hope. He was still, but not because he had given up. Because he had hope and believed help was coming. The Bible says, “Be still, and know that I am God.” That doesn’t mean we stop caring. It means we stop trying to be in control. That verse isn’t just for people who live quiet lives. It’s for people like us. The ones who are tired. The ones who are hurting. The ones who’ve tried everything, and now… maybe it’s time to stop and sit for a while.

We don’t know what that looks like for you. But we are learning that being still doesn’t mean we have failed. It just means we are learning to trust. Trust in the Lord. Trust that the Lord will take care of us. And maybe, when life feels too hard, trusting the Lord and being still is enough for today. 6

We are sure there have been times in our lives when we have ran ahead of God. We have hastedly made decisions before we prayed. We have tried to fix things on our own. We tell ourselves we were being “proactive,” but really, we were impatient and maybe a little scared to wait. We lacked patience. We lacked trust. And then there were other times when we stopped walking altogether. We got stuck. Completely lost. We didn’t know where to go, so we just sat down, like a dog that refuses to move in the middle of a sidewalk.

But the times we remember best were the times we followed the Lord closely. Not perfectly; we are human after all. And not always happily. But we stayed near the Lord as best as we could. We didn’t try to control everything. We can’t anyway. We just walked, one step at a time, next to the One who knew the way. 7 Dogs don’t need to know where we’re going. They just need to know we know. That’s enough for them.

Faith is like that. It’s not about having our own map, figuring out our destination, and then coming up with a plan to get there. Sure, we need to do our part, but faith is about knowing and trusting the One who’s leading. And us following. There was a woman who was going through something really difficult. She was homeless and sick, but she still came to church on Sundays. She said she felt safer there, even if it was just for an hour.

One day, she was asked how she kept showing up with such peace. She said, “I just try to stay close to the Lord. God walks ahead of me. I just follow.” The Bible says, “My sheep hear My voice, and I know them, and they follow Me.” That’s from Jesus. He’s asking us to stay close to Him. To listen to His voice. To follow His lead. That’s what we see in good dogs, the ones who walk right beside us, matching our pace, keeping their eyes on us, trusting that wherever we’re going… it’s going to be okay.

Don’t we want to live like that? To walk like that. Close to the Lord. Quietly. Trusting in the Lord. Faithfully. Not trying to rush off somewhere. Not running away. Just following the Lord. Right next to the Lord. 8 Because honestly, we don’t need to know everything about the road ahead. We just need to know the Lord knows the road ahead and that we are not walking alone. 9

There’s a verse that says, “Let your words be few.” Another says, “A gentle answer turns away anger.” Jesus Himself often stayed silent when people attacked Him. But when He did speak it was always on purpose. Always with truth. Always with love. That’s what we must learn. To stay quiet when words would only add noise. But to speak, clearly, purposefully, with love, when it helps someone else see what’s true. A good dog doesn’t bark to show off. A good dog barks to protect. To warn. To say, “Hey, something’s not right.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Return — No Matter How Far We've Wandered
excerpts.text: But when his name was called, even from way across the field, he’d pause. Sometimes he’d hesitate. Sometimes he’d give that look. Really? Right now? But then, every time, he’d come running. That’s the kind of heart we want to have with God. One that hears His voice, even when we’ve wandered far. A heart that turns back, even if it takes a second. The truth is, we have had seasons where we didn’t respond right away. Where we ignored the gentle nudge. Where we told ourselves we’d come back later. But the longer we waited, the harder it felt to return. Not because God had changed his mind or moved away; He is always waiting for us to return, with arms wide open. Always.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Return — No Matter How Far We've Wandered
excerpts.text: 12 God never stops calling. Even when we mess up. Even when we shut ourselves in. Even when we go too far. He calls us by name. Not with anger, but with kindness. Not to shame us, but to bring us back to Him. Jesus once told a story about a lost sheep. It wandered off. And instead of yelling at it or waiting for it to return on its own, the shepherd went after it. When he found it, he didn’t scold it. With relief, thankfulness, and gladness, he brought it home.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Return — No Matter How Far We've Wandered
excerpts.text: That’s what God does. He calls us. And when we come, even with our heads down and feeling sorry, He gives us the biggest, warmest bear hug. He does not guilt trip us. He does not lecture us. He just embraces us with a love we will not fully understand this side of heaven. Dogs instinctively know something about that. Probably. They might wander. But when they hear their name, they come running. Let’s be like that. Because no matter how far we’ve gone, wouldn’t we rather be near Him than anywhere else?

Life can be challenging. Life can hurt. Not all the time, maybe, but often enough. Things go wrong. Bodies break down. Plans fall apart. People let us down. And yet, somehow, we’re still here. And if we’re still here, maybe there’s a way to show a little joy. A little hope. Even if it’s just a small wag. Many of us have experienced or are currently experiencing challenging situations. We are living in shelters. Or are at hospitals recovering. Some of us are living in tents or in our cars. Yet, we can still smile.

14 Not because everything is fine. But because we can decide not to let the pain take away everything good. We can decide that we will continue to be joyful. Be kind. Be joyful. To laugh. That doesn’t mean we pretend everything’s okay when it’s not. It doesn’t mean we fake a smile or hide how we feel. It just means… even here, even now, we can still be thankful. For something. Like a warm cup of coffee. A friend who visits us at the hospital. A place to sit, rest, and just breathe. A word from Scripture that reminds us that we are still loved.

Dogs wag when they’re happy, yes. But sometimes they wag when they’re hurting, too. They wag because they know that the person who is near them cares for them. Because they trust the one who is with them. That’s the kind of joy. Not a loud kind of joy. And it does not have to be perfect, at first. But it does need to be real. The Bible says, “Rejoice in the Lord always.” Impossible? Think of it as more like wagging. Even in the middle of all the mess, all the pain, we can be glad that the Lord is here with us through it all.

Let’s learn. To look for the small things, the things that we take for granted that are still good. Like the sun on our face. The air we breathe. The fact that we can actually breathe. That we have an appetite. Are able to taste good food and say, “Oh, that’s good.” Joy can live alongside pain. Even on the hardest days. Go ahead and wag anyway. Because the Lord is with us. 15

Maybe that’s what God wants from us too. The Bible says that real religion, the kind God cares about, is looking after orphans and widows. It’s about helping those who can’t help themselves. It’s about caring for people most others ignore. 16 People in shelters and on sidewalks don’t have much, but they still look out for one another. They share food. Sometimes they even take turns keeping watch at night. They remember who’s sick, who’s scared, who needs an extra blanket.

Jesus did the same. He was always stopping for the ones no one noticed: the blind man calling from the side of the road, the woman who touched His robe in a crowd, the children the disciples tried to keep quiet. Jesus saw them. He saw their hearts. He stood with them. He shared in their suffering. He guarded them, even when others didn’t. Let’s be more like that. Let’s be someone who sees those who are invisible to the world. Someone who steps in to protect those who are weak. Someone who says, “Not this one. Not today.”

We live in a world that forgets the weak. But dogs remind us and Jesus shows us that grace is never just for the strong. Grace is all the more for the weak, those who are hurting. They are the ones who appreciate grace down to their aching hearts. Let’s slow down. Notice the invisibles. Guard the weak, the way the Lord guards us. 17

Dogs are good at just being near us. They don’t try to cheer us up with advice. They don’t leave when things get awkward or strange. They simply stay. A dog that quietly stays with us is a gift, especially when we’re hurting. I remember sitting beside someone in a hospital once. There wasn’t much I could do. There was absolutely nothing I could do. No way to make the situation better. No perfect words. Not one. Just a chair and a little bit of time. I sat. I stayed. I prayed in my heart. And when I got up to leave, he said quietly, “Thanks for being here.”

Sometimes what people need most isn’t a solution. It’s not the right words or an action plan. It’s just someone who doesn’t walk away. 18 Jesus did that. He stayed with people who were hurting. He touched the ones everyone else avoided. He cried with His friends. He didn’t run from pain. Not their pain, and not ours. The Bible says, “The Lord is close to the brokenhearted.” It doesn’t say He fixes everything right away. It says He’s close.

Don’t we all want to be like that? Stay close when someone feels alone. Even if we can’t fix the situation. Even if we don’t know what to say. Even if we can’t do anything to make things better. Dogs don’t always understand what we’re going through. But they know when to stay close. That’s a kind of love. Not just showing up when it’s easy, but sticking around when it is difficult. We don’t have to have all the answers. We don’t need answers at all. When things get hard, it’s easy to disappear, but if we want to love we need to stick around. We need to stay, just like the Lord stays with us, especially through hard times.

That the dogs trust their owners is admirable. Because we don’t always trust like that. We worry God might have forgotten about us. Might not take care of our needs. So we make backup plans. We check our wallets even when we just checked it five minutes ago. We worry. We want to believe God will take care of us, but sometimes it’s hard. Especially when things don’t go the way we think they should. When things run low. Our checking accounts. Food in the fridge. Time. Energy. Hope. Especially when I’ve been waiting a while.

One morning, he was asked how he stayed so calm. He said, “I’ve gone to bed hungry plenty of times. But God hasn’t forgotten me.” That is trust. In the Bible, Jesus tells His followers not to worry about food or clothes. He says the birds don’t store up food, and the flowers don’t work for beauty. But God feeds and clothes them anyway. Then He says, “Aren’t you worth more than they are?” God tells us we are. It’s not always easy to believe that. But don’t we believe what Jesus is saying is true? God gives us what we need. Maybe not everything we ask for, but enough to keep going. Not as soon as possible, but in God’s perfect timing. He gives us enough to remind us we’re not forgotten. And sometimes much more than enough.

Dogs trust their person to care for them. They sit near the bowl. They wait. Sometimes patiently and for longer than they would like. They don’t understand where the food comes from. But they know who gives it. Who fills up their bowl with good food. 21 Isn’t that the kind of trusting heart we want? A simple heart full of thanksgiving. A trusting heart that trusts the Hand that feeds us. We are all learning. To worry less. To ask simply. To wait with hope. Because even when we don’t see it yet, we believe the bowl will be filled by our good Lord.

Dogs can sleep almost anywhere. Haven’t we seen them stretched out on sidewalks? Curled up in noisy shelters. Tucked under tables during thunderstorms. Sometimes they sleep with one eye open, but still they sleep. And I’ve wondered how they do that. How can they rest in places that feel so uncertain? Part of the answer is simple: they trust who they’re with. They know someone is watching over them. And that’s enough. More than enough for them to sleep in peace.

There are dogs that sometimes sleep at our feet. Whenever we move, they lift their heads. Just a little, to see if we were still there. Then they would sigh and settle back down. As long as we were near, they could sleep. Haven’t there been been plenty of nights in our lives when we just couldn’t fall sleep? It might have been stress. Fear. Pain. Pain in our hearts. Pain in our bodies. Thoughts that run wild after the lights go out. The body might have been tired, but the mind kept spinning. It’s hard to rest when the world feels like it’s falling apart. Or when our own lives feel too uncertain.

23 But in the Psalms, there’s a verse that says, “I will lie down and sleep in peace, for You alone, Lord, make me dwell in safety.” It doesn’t say the Lord made the world a peaceful place. It doesn’t say the problems went away. It just says: the Lord is with us. We are safe. We can rest now. Dogs understand this in their own way. They don’t control much. They don’t know what tomorrow holds. But when they know they’re cared for, they are able to sleep. Not because their lives are perfect. But because they believe they’re protected and taken care of by their person.

Rest is not just something the body needs. It’s something the heart needs, too. And sometimes, the bravest thing we can do at the end of a hard day is to close our eyes and trust that God will take care of whatever we are worrying about. So, let’s stop carrying everything and put our worries down, at the foot of our Lord. Let’s stop rehearsing all the what-ifs. Instead, let’s breathe. Physically and spiritually. And be still. And sleep, like someone who is safely loved. Because we are loved.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Move On from What We Can't Change
excerpts.text: They don’t carry their regrets. They don’t replay it. They leave the past behind. And they keep walking. Don’t we all wish we were more like that? We carry things. Sometimes more than we should. Old regrets. Words we said and wish we hadn’t. Moments we replay over and over, trying to fix what’s already finished. But the past doesn’t change, no matter how long we carry it with us. And the more we stare at it, the harder it is to see what’s ahead.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Move On from What We Can't Change
excerpts.text: 25 There’s a verse in the Bible that says, “Forget the former things. Do not dwell on the past. See, I am doing a new thing.” Dwell. That word means to live in something. To stay there. And we don’t want to live in old mistakes anymore. We want to live in what God is doing now. Let’s learn how to let go. Not to forget completely, but to stop holding on so tight. To trust that God’s mercy and grace really does mean we are forgiven and loved. And that even when we mess up, God doesn’t walk away. He still calls us with open arms.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Move On from What We Can't Change
excerpts.text: Dogs live in the moment. They mess up, but then after a little while they look for what’s next. They keep wagging. They keep walking. Leave the past with the One who can carry it. Stop staring backward. Look forward. Walk ahead. We are forgiven. We have been unchained. We are loved. And we are not alone. 26

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Focus on What's Worth Following
excerpts.text: Ever wonder what that is for us? Some days, we seek comfort. Other days, it’s control. Sometimes we go after people’s approval. Hoping they’ll like us or notice what we’ve done. And there are days we don’t even know what we want. We just feel tired from all the running after things. But deep down, we all want something real. Something that lasts. Something that’s worth the effort. Jesus said, “Seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Focus on What's Worth Following
excerpts.text: 27 He wasn’t saying we can’t care about other things like food, clothes, and what will happen tomorrow. But what Jesus was saying is: run after the right thing first. Dogs know how to run. Unleash them at a park and most dogs run. But the best ones know when to run and where to run. Go after what really matters. Not just what’s in front of us. Spend our time and energy on the things that bring life. Loving people. Serving others and serving God. Walking with God.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning to Focus on What's Worth Following
excerpts.text: There’s so much in the world pulling for our attention: screens, headlines, pressure to do more, have more, be more, and more screens. But peace comes when we follow the One who gives real peace. Slow down. Look at what we are chasing after. If it isn’t something that will give us what really matters, stop. And then run after God and His kingdom. 28

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning Where We Belong and Who's Waiting There
excerpts.text: And when they arrive, they don’t knock. They don’t ask if it’s okay. They come in, wag their tail, curl up in their bed, and fall asleep like they never left. That’s a kind of trust we see often with dogs, but us? Not as often. Life can feel like wandering. Like walking through places that aren’t home. Like seeking things that we thought was going to fill us with joy, and peace, and love, but does the complete opposite: leave us empty. Waiting for a voice to say, “You can stop running now.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning Where We Belong and Who's Waiting There
excerpts.text: Jesus told a story about a son who ran away. He spent everything he had. He made a mess of his life. And when he finally decided to go back home, tired, scared, and not sure what to expect, his father ran out to meet him. 29 His father didn’t yell at him. He wasn’t mad at him. He didn’t punish him. He threw his arms around him and said, “Welcome home.” That’s what God is like. No matter how far we’ve gone, He still wants us home. And not just someday. He wants us home right now. He wants to give us His peace, now. He gives us a deep sense that we are not lost anymore.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning Where We Belong and Who's Waiting There
excerpts.text: Home isn’t always a place. But it’s always a Person. It’s where love meets us at the door, of a place, of our hearts, and says, “You’re safe here.” And when this life is done , when all the noise fades and the walking is over, He will meet us again. And even if we have a long list of things we got wrong, He will remind us that He forgave us, for every item on that list and a whole bunch more that we forgot about. He will take us in with open arms and a smile that says, “Welcome. You made it. I love you.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning Where We Belong and Who's Waiting There
excerpts.text: Dogs know something about that. They don’t worry about what they’ve done. They just run home. Stop running in circles. Listen for His call. Go home, to the One who’s been waiting all along. 30 Final Thoughts The Walk Isn’t Over If you’ve made it to the end of these lessons, thank you. That means you stayed. You listened. You walked a little further with us. We didn’t write this book because we had all the answers. We wrote it because we’re still learning, too. Learning how to trust. How to rest. How to follow. How to stay close to the One who never leaves us.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning Where We Belong and Who's Waiting There
excerpts.text: Dogs have taught us more than we expected. Not through big moments, but in the small ones. In the way they wait. In the way they stay close. In the way they forgive easily and love without holding back. We believe God does that, too. Only deeper. Only truer. And if you’re reading this while walking through something hard such as sickness, loss, fear, loneliness we hope these simple stories reminded you of something real: You are not forgotten. You are not too far gone. And you are never, ever alone.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Learning Where We Belong and Who's Waiting There
excerpts.text: 31 The walk isn’t over yet. There’s more to learn. More to love. More to seek. But for now, we pray you find peace in the presence of the One who walks beside you. Step by step. Wag by wag. Grace upon grace. 32

4 Some chapters may make you laugh. Others might stir something deeper. All of them are here to remind you that the way of Jesus is still possible, even in a world that often forgets Him. So take your time. Read slowly. Or fast if you want to. But let these little stories settle into your mind and into your heart. And remember: we don’t need to become louder to be faithful. We just need to stay close to the One who leads with truth, grace, and peace.

Not to whoever had the best snack. Not to whoever seemed more fun. Not to the loudest voice. Or the sweetest voice. But to his people. In politics that kind of loyalty is almost exinct. These days, we’re trained to treat loyalty like a party favor. Easy to give. Easy to take back. It shifts with the headlines, bends with the polling numbers, and disappears the moment it’s inconvenient. 6 But the loyalty God calls us to is different. It doesn’t bow to platforms. It doesn’t pledge allegiance to personalities. It stands still — eyes on the door — no matter what.

In the Bible, we meet people like Daniel who served in a pagan government yet refused to abandon his devotion to God. When the law said “worship the king,” Daniel went home, opened his windows, and prayed anyway. Not as a protest. Not to make a scene. But because he was loyal — to the One who had never left him. We need more Daniels. Not more partisans. Not more tribalists. Not more people addicted to “our side winning.” We need people who stay loyal to God, even when their party forgets Him.

Let’s be honest — no political party owns the truth. No candidate is perfect. Far from. Every human system is flawed. That’s why God didn’t ask us to pledge allegiance to a governmetn system or to a party. He commanded us to follow Him. To be holy. To love mercy. To act justly. To walk humbly. That kind of loyalty is hard, in fact impossible without God. But it’s the only kind that lasts. 7 Just like that dog at the door — still, quiet, focused — the world may not understand why we don’t jump at every command or chase every treat. But we’re not here for applause. We’re here for faithfulness.

It was the kind of bark that meant something. She wasn’t afraid. She wasn’t panicking. She just knew what was right. We need that kind of bark today. The book of Proverbs says, “A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger.” (Proverbs 15:1) And yet, many of us have gotten used to harsh words. Loud tweets. Angry headlines. Pretend rage dressed up as passion for justice. But yelling isn’t the same as righteousness. And barking doesn’t mean you’re right.

Jesus didn’t waste words. He spoke clearly, truthfully — and always for the right reasons. When He overturned the tables in the temple, it wasn’t out of outrage addiction. It was holy zeal. It was love defending holiness. That’s the difference. 10 Dogs don’t bark for attention. They bark because they care. They bark because something isn’t right. They bark because someone they love might be in danger. So ask yourself: When I speak up, am I doing it out of love or pride? Is it conviction — or ego?

If danger came, the watchman would sound the alarm. If danger came and he stayed silent, people would get hurt. If he spoke up, even if no one listened, he had done his part. Some of us have stopped watching. 12 We think someone else will guard the gate. Someone smarter. Louder. More important. But what if God placed you where you are for a reason? You don’t have to guard everything. But you do need to stay alert. In your home. Your school. Your church. Watch what comes in. And pay attention to what lingers.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Don't follow the loudest voice — follow the true voice. A political parallel about not being swayed by charisma.
excerpts.text: Jesus once said, “My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me.” (John 10:27) He was talking about us. He wanted us to know Him well enough that when He speaks — we recognize it. And when He doesn’t — we wait. 14 In politics, this is hard. Every side says they speak for truth. Every leader says they care. But the more we listen to the crowd, the harder it is to hear the Shepherd.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Don't follow the loudest voice — follow the true voice. A political parallel about not being swayed by charisma.
excerpts.text: So how do we tell the difference? A stranger’s voice will always pull you away. God’s voice draws you near. The stranger rushes. Pressures. Flatters. The Shepherd leads. Gently. Firmly. Always with love. You don’t need to know every answer. But you do need to know the One who does. And that means spending time with Him. Listening in to His voice, quietly. Reading His words. Chewing on the meaning. Letting the Holy Spirit shape your heart. The better you know His voice, the less tempted you’ll be to follow another.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Distraction is the enemy of discernment. Politicians thrive on distraction; Christians must stay focused.
excerpts.text: We argue. We worry. We pick sides. And before long, we’ve forgotten what we were supposed to be doing in the first place. The Bible says, “Set your minds on things above, not on earthly things.” (Colossians 3:2) That’s hard to do when you’re sprinting after every distraction. But staying focused doesn’t mean you don’t care. It means you care enough not to waste your energy on noise. 16 Dogs chase squirrels because it’s instinct. People chase them because it feels like action; we feel like we’re doing something.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Distraction is the enemy of discernment. Politicians thrive on distraction; Christians must stay focused.
excerpts.text: But real wisdom isn’t about reacting to everything. It’s about knowing what matters — and letting the rest go. Jesus wasn’t easily distracted. When people tried to trap Him with clever questions or emotional arguments, He didn’t take the bait. He saw right through the noise and spoke to the heart of the issue. Every time. You don’t have to respond to everything. You don’t have to fix every argument. And you definitely don’t have to chase every squirrel. Some things are better left in the tree.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Engage, but don't get filthy. A lesson in clean confrontation — truth with grace.
excerpts.text: But the moment you roll in the mud, you lose more than your position — you lose your integrity, your witness. 18 The apostle Paul once wrote, “Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good.” (Romans 12:21) That’s not a slogan. That’s a command. The world already has enough mud. What it needs are people willing to stay clean — even when it’s messy. That doesn’t mean you need to always stay quiet. It doesn’t mean you ignore injustice. It means you answer with truth — not with tricks.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Engage, but don't get filthy. A lesson in clean confrontation — truth with grace.
excerpts.text: Jesus could’ve out-argued every Pharisee. Easily. But He never stooped to their level. He didn’t roll in their mud. He humbly and gently stood firm in the light of truth. When the world plays dirty, shine brighter. When politics gets ugly, speak with grace. You don’t have to match the mess to make a difference. You just have to stay clean — even if you’re the only one who does. 19

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: There's a difference between vigilance and paranoia. A good dog does not panic — it watches and waits.
excerpts.text: But choices made out of fear are almost always bad choices. It rushes. It exaggerates. It forgets that God is still in charge. He has always been and will always be. Jesus once told His followers to stay awake — to be ready. But He never told them to panic. In fact, again and again, He said, “Do not be afraid.” 20 We were never meant to live with our hearts clenched. Being faithful doesn’t mean being frightened. Quite the opposite: it means keeping your eyes open and your feet steady.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: There's a difference between vigilance and paranoia. A good dog does not panic — it watches and waits.
excerpts.text: Like a good dog watching the gate — not pacing, not growling, just… watching. Trust that when it’s time to act, the Master will let you know. And until then, you can sit quietly. Because you’re not guarding the world on your own. You’re just doing your part. And that’s enough. 21

And sometimes, we do the same. We say we’re serving God, but deep down we’re really serving ourselves. We want approval. Or control. Or just the thrill of being important and everyone listening to you. 22 But the leash tells the truth. In the end, someone is guiding you — shaping your choices, steering your reactions, leading your heart. Jesus said, “No one can serve two masters.” (Matthew 6:24) You can’t follow God and follow your ego. You can’t walk in truth and be led by fear. Sooner or later, one will pull harder.

That’s why it matters who’s holding the leash. Let it be the One who loves you. The One who doesn’t yank you or trick you or push you. The One who walks beside you and knows where you’re suppose to be going — even when you have no clue. Because when God leads, you don’t have to run wild. You just have to follow. 23

excerpts.lesson_title: When To Sit And When To Stand
excerpts.text: There are times in life when obedience means sitting still. But there are also times when faithfulness means standing up. 24 In politics, people argue about when to resist and when to comply. Some shout at every little thing. Others stay silent, even when something deeply wrong is happening. But wisdom is not about picking a side. Wisdom is about listening to God. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego didn’t stand up to everything. They served a foreign king. They did their work. But when they were told to bow to a false god, they stood. Quietly and calmly. It was not about hate, but about love for God. They knew who they served. And that made the choice simple.

excerpts.lesson_title: When To Sit And When To Stand
excerpts.text: Sometimes faith looks like waiting patiently. Other times it looks like refusing to move. The difference isn’t pride. It’s purpose. A dog doesn’t stand to prove something. He stands to protect. So before you act, ask: “Is this about me — or about what’s right?” If you walk closely with the Lord, you’ll know when to sit. And when it’s time to stand. You won’t need anyone’s permission. You’ll just know. 25

excerpts.lesson_title: Be A Shepherd, Not A Politician
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Care for the flock — don't just court votes. Pastoral responsibility over political ambition.
excerpts.text: Jesus said, “The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.” (John 10:11) Was Jesus talking about a job? No. He was talking about love. A love that stems from deep within the heart. A love that is steady, watchful, protective. The kind of love that doesn’t quit when it gets hard. 26 Too many leaders today chase approval instead of people. They run campaigns, not care. They protect their positions, not the vulnerable. But shepherding doesn’t need a title. You don’t have to be famous, have lots of important-sounding titles or degrees to watch over the ones near you. You just need to notice them. And stay close to them.

excerpts.lesson_title: Be A Shepherd, Not A Politician
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Care for the flock — don't just court votes. Pastoral responsibility over political ambition.
excerpts.text: The best dogs never look like they’re doing much. But they’re always aware. Always ready. Always nearby. You don’t have to win arguments or gather votes to make a difference. Just look after the ones God gave you. That’s what a good shepherd does. 27

He greeted everyone with a wag. Didn’t matter who you were, or how your day had gone. If you walked through that door, you were met with kindness. That wag wasn’t just excitement. Yes, he was excited, but it was also a welcome. There’s something healing about being seen, welcomed — not for what you’ve done, not for what you believe, not for how smart you are or how much you have, but simply because you’re human. Dogs seem to understand that much better than we do.

Politics, on the other hand, tends to sort people into camps: Us versus Them. Right versus. Wrong. Blue versus. Red. Win versus Lose. But people aren’t sides. People are souls, precious souls. And many of them are wounded. 28 They’ve been hurt by words, or the lack of words, left out of conversations, judged by labels. Some… many don’t trust anymore. And some are just tired. When Jesus walked the earth, He didn’t check credentials before healing. He didn’t quiz people on what they understood. He didn’t require that you be on this side or that side. He saw the heart first. He saw the wound — and moved toward it.

That’s what mercy does. It doesn’t mean we ignore the truth. It means we carry the truth but with gentleness and humility. The world doesn’t need more shouting. It needs more wagging — small signs of welcome. A look that says, “You matter.” A tone that says, “You’re safe here, even if we disagree.” Kindness won’t fix everything. But it opens the door. And sometimes, that’s all we need for healing to begin. 29

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: God's truth is still truth, even when visibility is low.
excerpts.text: It’s hard to know who to trust when you can’t see what’s next. But God isn’t lost in the fog. He’s still guiding us, and the entire universe. We may not feel Him, but God is still near. And as He has always been, He is still good, and good to us. The Bible says, “We walk by faith, not by sight.” (2 Corinthians 5:7) That isn’t just about believing big things — it’s about trusting God for all the small things in our lives, especially when the way forward isn’t clear.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: God's truth is still truth, even when visibility is low.
excerpts.text: 30 You don’t need to see everything to stay faithful. Often it’s just the opposite: our faith grows when we don’t see much at all. You just need to know Who you’re walking with. Or Who is walking with us. Sometimes that means standing still. Sometimes that means taking one small, careful step. But always, it means listening for His voice. The world will try to rush you. Push you. Confuse you. But you don’t have to move until the Shepherd puts out His hand for yours and says, “Come.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: God's truth is still truth, even when visibility is low.
excerpts.text: So wait in the fog if you have to. Not with fear, but with faith. Because He hasn’t forgotten you. And He’s never late. 31 Final Thoughts Politics can make us feel like we always have to pick a side, raise our voice, or brace for a fight. But maybe that’s not the only way. Maybe we don’t have to match the noise. Maybe we don’t have to chase every issue, prove every point, or win every argument. Maybe what the world really needs — what God is asking of us — is something simpler.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: God's truth is still truth, even when visibility is low.
excerpts.text: Loyalty that doesn’t shift with the headlines. Kindness that continues, even when people disagree. A voice that speaks truth, in love, and onlywhen it’s needed. And the courage to stand still when the fog is thick. We’ve learned a lot from dogs in these pages. They don’t pretend to know everything. They’re not experts or influencers. But they know how to stay close by. They know how to listen, carefully. They know how to wait, sometimes for a long time. They know how to be welcoming. And some it seems know how to love.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: God's truth is still truth, even when visibility is low.
excerpts.text: They guard what matters. They don’t give up when things get tough. And they’re don’t distracted by the crowd. Well, the good ones anyway. That’s what we’re called to be — faithful. Not loud, but quiet and steady. Not driven by fear, but filled by His love and led by the One who sees clearly, even when we can’t. 32 So if you ever feel overwhelmed by the state of the world, take a breath. Look up. Remember who you belong to. You don’t have to carry it all. You can’t, even if you tried. But you can be faithful with what’s right in front of you.

The dog walked beside him to the car, no leash needed. He knew the routine. More importantly, he knew his person. 9 This was faithfulness: showing up, staying close, building trust one ordinary day at a time. The dog did it instinctively. Humans had to choose it. Every morning. Every client. Every tax season. The accountant realized God looks for the same thing. Not the loudest or most impressive. Those who take what they’re given—a task, a team, a calling—and remain faithful with it. Those who work as if for the Lord himself.

The dog climbed into the car and settled in his spot. Tomorrow would be the same. 6:45 AM. Circle three times. Lie down. Be there. Sometimes the most powerful ministry happens in the quietest faithfulness. Sometimes just showing up changes everything. 10

14 Even leaving was about patience. About reading the moment. About moving only when movement made sense. The director thought about all the deals he’d rushed into. All the opportunities he’d chased because everyone else was chasing them. All the doors he’d tried to force open. Maybe the owner and his old dog understood something he was just beginning to learn: True strength isn’t in the speed of your response. It’s in knowing when response is actually needed. Sometimes the best business strategy looks like an old dog by his owner’s desk: calm, watchful, ready to move when the moment is right, and completely still until then.

In business, as in faith, patience isn’t passive. It’s active trust. It’s watching carefully while others panic. It’s being ready while others rush. It’s knowing that the One who controls all outcomes has perfect timing. The dog had learned it instinctively. Now the director was beginning to understand: Sometimes the most powerful move is to wait. 15

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.
excerpts.text: A year later, at the company Christmas party, someone mentioned they’d been featured in a regional construction magazine as “the company everyone’s talking about but no one’s heard of.” 19 The foreman raised his beer. “To good work and good people.” Duke wandered through the party, tail constantly moving, greeting everyone with the same enthusiasm. Happy to be there. Happy to be included. Needing no recognition beyond the occasional pat on the head. The architect understood now. Pride announces itself. Joy just radiates. And in the end, the thing that radiates travels further than the thing that shouts.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.
excerpts.text: Over the years, the owner had learned to read Sailor’s signals. A certain way he’d position himself when a new employee wasn’t trustworthy. How he’d linger by the tables of regular customers who were having hard days, staying close when he’d usually return to his spot. The way he’d become restless when equipment was about to fail. Some called it instinct. The owner knew better. Discernment wasn’t about being suspicious or 24 paranoid. It was about caring enough to notice. Loving enough to pay attention.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.
excerpts.text: The owner quietly told the kitchen to take extra precautions with that order. Fresh gloves. Separate prep area. Double-check everything. The meal went perfectly. The elderly woman kept it down. First full meal she’d enjoyed in weeks. 26 Discernment isn’t about finding problems. It’s about preventing harm. It’s about loving people enough to notice what might hurt them. Like a Portuguese Water Dog with centuries of working heritage, catching what others miss, sitting down and refusing to move when something’s not right.

“You probably can, eventually. But first you follow. You learn why things are done certain ways. Then you can make real improvements, ones that actually work and stick.” Blue was asleep between them now, completely at peace. Years of following the same routes, the same person, the same patterns. Not because he couldn’t think for himself, but because he’d learned that wisdom comes from walking beside someone who knows the way. Scripture says, “Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.” Simple. Direct. Follow first, understand later.

Instead, she’d trained him to read situations. To position himself between threat and vulnerable. To use his size as a shield, not a weapon. 40 A month earlier, during inventory, an elderly employee had fallen in the stockroom. Tank had immediately lain down beside him, letting the man use his solid body to sit up, then stand. Tank stayed quiet and still, being what was needed: something stable to lean on. “People see a Rottweiler and think violence,” the manager told the cashier as they closed that evening. “But real strength isn’t about attacking. It’s about knowing when someone needs protection and quietly providing it.”

Scripture says the strong ought to bear with the weak. Tank lived it every day. He could have 42 dominated any space he entered. Instead, he made himself a shelter. By closing time, the teenage cashier had handled her second Saturday successfully. As she counted down her register, Tank sat beside her, patient and protective. “I used to be scared of big dogs,” she told the manager. “Most people are. They see the size and assume danger.” “But he’s the opposite,” the cashier said. “He makes me feel safe.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.
excerpts.text: After feeding him, she sat back at her desk. The emails had multiplied overnight. Dozens of new messages. Each one urgent. Winston finished eating, walked to his bed in the sunny spot by the window, circled three times, and lay down. Within minutes, he was asleep again. She looked at him fondly. “How do you do it? How can you sleep so much?” But as she watched, she noticed something. When the doorbell rang with a delivery, Winston was instantly alert, checking who was there. When it was safe, he went back to sleep. When she got up to get coffee, he watched until she sat down, then closed his eyes again. He wasn’t lazy. He was conserving energy for when it mattered.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.
excerpts.text: 47 She hung up. Closed her laptop. Winston opened one eye, watching her. “You’re right,” she told him. “Rest isn’t what you do when the work is done. It’s what you do so the work can get done.” She lay down on the couch. Winston waddled over and curled up on the floor beside her. For the first time in weeks, she slept. Really slept. She’d left the laptop at her desk, put her phone in airplane mode, and literally fell asleep within minutes.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.
excerpts.text: A new client called, demanding a rush job over the weekend. “I don’t work weekends,” she said. “This is urgent!” 49 She looked at Winston, deeply asleep in his afternoon spot. “Everything’s urgent until it’s not. I’ll look at it Monday.” “But—“ “Monday.” She hung up. Winston’s tail thumped once against the floor, though his eyes stayed closed. Scripture says, “Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.” Winston lived it. Rest wasn’t his reward for being a good dog. It was how he stayed a good dog.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.
excerpts.text: “Wisdom isn’t knowing when to run,” he told his son, who was learning the business. “It’s knowing when to wait and when waiting turns into the right time to move.” Pepper was teaching the same lesson to a young stray dog that had shown up at the farm. The younger dog wanted to chase everything immediately. Pepper would hold him back with his body, making him wait, showing him the difference between frantic activity and purposeful action. Scripture says there’s a time for everything. A time to plant and a time to harvest. A time to wait and a time to run.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.
excerpts.text: 61 “I had a professor who never let us forget our mistakes,” the new vet said. “He said it would make us better vets.” “Did it?” “No. It just made us afraid to try.” The tech nodded. “I’ve been doing this for years. You want to know the secret to lasting in this field?” The new vet looked up. “Learn from your mistakes, then let them go. Like Honey with that cat. She’ll probably never chase another cat in the clinic. But she’s not going to spend the rest of her life feeling guilty about it either.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.
