excerpts.text: The new vet found Honey in the recovery room, lying beside the waking patient. “You know what I learned?” she told the tech later. “That first surgery that went wrong? It taught me what to watch for. If I hadn’t made that mistake, I might not have caught the problem today.” The tech smiled. “Mistakes are just experience in disguise.” Months passed. The new vet grew more confident. She still made mistakes, as everyone did, but she’d learned to acknowledge them, learn from them, and move forward.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.
excerpts.text: The clinic’s philosophy had become clear over time: excellence wasn’t about perfection. It was about learning, growing, and not letting fear of failure prevent them from trying. Honey demonstrated this daily. She’d learned not to chase cats in the clinic, but she still played with them at home. She’d learned to avoid the vaccine fridge, but still checked the break room for crumbs. Each mistake had taught her something without diminishing her spirit. “Grace,” the tech said one day, watching Honey comfort a scared puppy getting its first shots. “That’s what she has. Grace to forgive herself and others.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.
excerpts.text: The new vet, now mentoring the student, understood. In veterinary medicine, as in life, you’d make mistakes. Some small, some significant. The difference between those who thrived and those who burned out wasn’t skill or intelligence. It was the ability to accept grace: for yourself and others. 65 Honey aged over the years but never lost her ability to shake off the bad moments. Even on her last day at the clinic, when arthritis made her movements slow, she knocked over a water bowl, looked embarrassed for a moment, then wagged her tail and moved on.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.
excerpts.text: That’s what stuck with everyone who worked there. What mattered was not the mistakes, but the recovery, not the failures, but the grace to try again. Scripture says we all fall short, but grace covers our shortcomings. Honey lived that truth every day, teaching a clinic full of perfectionists that healing, whether for animals or humans, began with forgiving ourselves. The clinic kept her collar hanging in the break room after she passed. Below it, a small sign: “Mistakes happen. Grace happens more.”

The next morning, he made a phone call. He asked for help and admitted he couldn’t carry it alone. Because sometimes the wisest thing we can do is stay with the pack. Even when it’s messy. Even when others move at different speeds. Even when we have to adjust our rhythm to match theirs. Scripture says it plainly: “Two are better than one, because they have a good return for their labor. If either of them falls down, one can help the other up.”

The three dogs knew this without being told. They knew that what matters isn’t being the strongest or the fastest or the smartest. What matters is staying together, adjusting to each other’s pace, and carrying each other when the load gets too heavy. Because together, we go farther than we ever could alone. 69

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.
excerpts.text: 71 Then Atlas and his handler walked on. This happened again and again over the years. Atlas was threatened, bumped, yelled at, even attacked by other dogs. Each time, he had the power to strike back. Each time, he had reason to defend himself. Each time, he chose not to. Because Atlas understood something that takes most creatures a lifetime to learn. Strength isn’t proved by using it. Real strength is knowing when to hold back, when to stay calm, when to refuse to let someone else’s aggression pull you into becoming aggressive too.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.
excerpts.text: Scripture says it plainly: “Do not repay evil with evil. Overcome evil with good.” Atlas knew this without being told. When threatened, the most powerful response isn’t always the one that strikes back. Sometimes it’s the one that stands firm, holds position, and simply refuses to bite. Because in the end, who we’re becoming matters. 72

Sky was a Golden Retriever who lived with an elderly woman in a small cottage. Every morning at six, Sky would sit by his bowl in the kitchen and wait. He never paced. He never whined. He never searched the floor for crumbs or tried to open the pantry door. He simply sat and waited. The woman’s routine varied each day. Sometimes she fed him at six-fifteen. Sometimes not until seven. On difficult mornings when her arthritis flared up, it might be even later. But Sky never panicked. He waited with quiet confidence because he knew she would feed him. She always had.

On the day they ran out of food, the woman sat at her kitchen table and cried. Sky walked over and rested his head on her knee. She looked down at him, this dog who had no idea about pension checks or bank accounts or bills, and yet somehow remained peaceful. That afternoon, a neighbor knocked on the door. She had bought too much dog food by mistake, she said. Would Sky like some? The woman accepted it gratefully, tears filling her eyes again.

Sky knew better. He understood what she was still learning. Scripture says it plainly: “Give us this day our daily bread.” Give us this day our daily bread: today’s provision, not tomorrow’s worry. Because the One who feeds the birds and clothes the flowers has never once forgotten His own. 75 Sky sat by his bowl every morning for years. Some mornings the food came early. Some mornings it came late. But it always came. Because provision doesn’t come from the paycheck or the pension or the deal we’re counting on. It comes from the Provider who has never missed a single day.

76 Final Thoughts Good business begins with a faithful heart. Business is more than numbers and plans and products. It’s people. It’s choices. It’s moments where character is revealed and where trust is either built or broken. Every deal, every deadline, every delay gives us a chance to show who we belong to. The world tells us to compete, to climb, to hustle harder, to push past people and chase the win. But good dogs remind us of something simpler: faithfulness, loyalty, stillness, and trust.

They show up, they listen, they guard, they forgive, they follow. They stay close to us. And in those small, quiet acts, they point to something greater. Someone greater. We walk with the same God who clothes the flowers and feeds the birds. He walks with us through boardrooms and job sites, offices and kitchens, early mornings and late nights. 77 God has not forgotten us. We are not left to carry this alone. Whether we’re leading companies or folding towels, starting over or holding on, may we work with the kind of faith that trusts the Master, loves the pack, and stays ready to move when He calls.

Jesus knew what it was like to be without a place. “Foxes have dens and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head.” — Luke 9:58 The Son of God walked this earth with nowhere to settle. He belonged everywhere and nowhere at once. And yet He came — for us. He gave up everything for us, for the overlooked, for those still searching for home. 6 One man shared: “I’m not just homeless. I’m human. I lost my house, not my heart.”

And isn’t that what we all hope for? “I have called you by name. You are mine.” — Isaiah 43:1 God sees who we are and whose we are. Even without a place, we are not without a Shepherd. The dog didn’t ask for much. A name. A little food. A place to lie down. And someone to stay with. That’s all most people need, too. And that’s what the Shepherd gives. 7

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: When the world is indifferent, God draws near.
excerpts.text: The cold doesn’t care who you are. The cold affects everyone and doesn’t care if you were once a business owner, a teacher, a nurse, someone who worked for a big company, or a child. It wraps around anyone it finds—without kindness or memory. But God is not like the cold. “He will cover you with His feathers, and under His wings you will find refuge.” — Psalm 91:4 When the cold starts to freeze your veins, God comes near.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: When the world is indifferent, God draws near.
excerpts.text: This verse isn’t just about clothes. It’s about seeing people — the way Jesus sees them — especially when the world turns away. Maybe you know what it feels like to be cold. Not just your skin, but your soul. Maybe life has numbed you. Maybe you’ve been brushed past or spoken over. Maybe you’re still trying to warm up after a long winter of loss. Please know this: “The Lord is close to the brokenhearted.” — Psalm 34:18 The cold may not care. But the Shepherd does.

You might be reading this and feel like no one sees you. Like you’ve been passed by more times than you can count. Maybe your life feels like a mess. Maybe you’ve been hurt by people who were supposed to help. Please hear this: God has never walked past you. Not once. He sees you. He stops. And He stays. 11 And if you’ve been the one to stop — maybe in a small way, maybe with tears in your own eyes — thank you.

Every time we pause for someone in pain, every time we sit and wait without judgment, every time we let our hearts be interrupted, we echo the heart of Jesus. Some dogs survive because someone stopped. Some people do, too. And maybe that’s part of how the Shepherd works. Through the slow hands of those who are willing to stop. To listen. To love. 12

13 But here’s what Scripture says: “Though my father and mother forsake me, the Lord will receive me.” — Psalm 27:10 When everyone else walks away, God does not. God draws closer. He does not leave when things get hard. He does not pull back when we are messed up, weak, or not who we used to be. He stays, close to us. And He carries us through. “Even to your old age and gray hairs I am He, I am He who will sustain you. I have made you and I will carry you.” — Isaiah 46:4

Maybe you’ve been left behind. Maybe by family, friends, or by your church. Maybe by a system that should have helped. Maybe by the version of life you once hoped for. But remember, you’re not forgotten. Not to God. He sees where you are hurting. He knows what you’ve lost. And He’s not waiting for you to be better. He moves toward you. Actually, He’s already here. God is saying to us, “Come to me. Come home.” God’s door is always open. All we have to do is open our own hearts, walk through, and be embraced by God. And we are home.

“She gave more than all the others.” — Luke 21:3 15 Why? Because she gave from her need. Not her extra. She gave out of love. There’s a myth we sometimes believe — that we need to have more than what we need before we can give. More money than we need. More time than we need. More security than we need. But the heart of God often works through what seems not much at all. “Five loaves and two fish.” — John 6:9

That’s all one boy had. And it was more than enough for thousands — when placed in the hands of Jesus. Maybe all you have today is half a sandwich. Maybe just a kind word. Maybe a quiet moment to sit beside someone who is struggling, in pain, and suffering. It might not feel like much. But in a world where people are passed over who have nothing, even crumbs can taste like a feast. “Whoever gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones… will surely not lose their reward.” — Matthew 10:42

16 A man used to say, “You’re never too poor to give something. Even if it’s just your place in line.” Because sometimes the greatest generosity isn’t what we give — but how we give it. Give without fear. Give without keeping score. Give without waiting to have more. With open hands. And an open heart. 17

That was years ago. But he still sat there — every afternoon — just in case. Faith doesn’t always look bold. Sometimes it looks like sitting, still. Like hoping when everyone else tells you to give up. Like believing love remembers you — even when you’ve been left in silence. 18 Jesus told a story about that kind of waiting. “But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son…” — Luke 15:20

The prodigal son had wandered far. But the father had never stopped watching the road. That’s what love does. It waits. It looks out the window. It keeps a light on. There’s a deeper kind of waiting, too. The kind that says: “Even if the person I long for never returns… I will still be ready to forgive. To welcome. To try again.” A woman once told us, “I’ve waited my whole life for someone to come back. But somewhere along the way, I realized… it’s God who’s been waiting for me.” That shift in perspective, toward the truth of God, the heart of God — that quiet awakening — changed her.

Not all waiting ends with a reunion. But all waiting can lead us back to the One who never stopped hoping for us. “The Lord is good to those who wait for Him, to the soul who seeks Him.” — Lamentations 3:25 19 The little dog on the side of the road hadn’t moved in hours. People said he’d been sitting there since morning. Someone must have promised to come back. And maybe they didn’t. But that dog still believed.

Some people only see the outside. The tangled hair. Rips in the clothes. Or the broken places. They see what’s missing. What’s wrong. What doesn’t look clean or safe or presentable. But God sees deeper. “The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.” — 1 Samuel 16:7 21 There was a man who had lost most of his teeth. His skin was worn, and his eyes were tired. People avoided him in public. He told us, “Sometimes I go days without hearing my own name.”

But one afternoon, a child came in with a meal for the shelter. And that child looked him in the eyes, smiled wide, and said, “Hi.” That child had no fear. He had no hesitation. All he had was kindness. He said later, “That one moment reminded me I’m still a person.” Jesus did that all the time. He touched lepers. He dined with outcasts. He called people by name when others called them unclean. He never saw people as problems to be fixed — but as souls to be loved.

“You knit me together in my mother’s womb… I am fearfully and wonderfully made.” — Psalm 139:13–14 We are not accidents. We are not our bruises. We are not what we’ve lost. We are not less human because we hurt. That black dog didn’t wag his tail for almost a week. He just watched. Waited. Listened. But the first time someone said, “You’re safe here,” something seemed to have changed. He curled up that night, not just because he was tired, but because he finally believed — he belonged.

excerpts.lesson_title: A Bowl of Water and a Name
excerpts.text: A volunteer at a shelter once made it a habit to learn everyone’s name. Even if someone had only been there one night, even if they didn’t want to talk, she’d remember. One man said, “You know what makes this place feel different? They don’t just serve soup. They remember and say my name.” We were never meant to live as nameless bodies moving through the world. We were created to be known — by God and by one another.

excerpts.lesson_title: A Bowl of Water and a Name
excerpts.text: “Fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name; you are mine.” — Isaiah 43:1 24 There is power in being called by your name. In being reminded that you are not invisible. Not forgotten. Not “just another homeless man.” Not “just a stray.” But someone. Someone worth remembering. One day Jesus passed through a crowd and stopped beneath a tree. There was a man hiding there — a tax collector named Zacchaeus, hated and avoided by many. But Jesus didn’t scold him. He said:

excerpts.lesson_title: A Bowl of Water and a Name
excerpts.text: “Zacchaeus, come down immediately. I must stay at your house today.” — Luke 19:5 He said his name. That was the beginning of Zacchaeus’ healing. Not the end of the story — but the start of coming home to God. Maybe you’ve gone a long time without hearing your name in a gentle voice. Maybe you’ve been called things that have hurt you. Maybe you’ve been overlooked, passed by, or treated like you don’t matter. But the Shepherd knows your name. And even now He is calling your name.

excerpts.lesson_title: A Bowl of Water and a Name
excerpts.text: Sometimes it’s not the big gestures that carry the most weight. It’s a bowl of water on a hot day. It’s someone remembering you like you matter. It’s being called “friend,” or “sir,” or “ma’am,” when the world expects no one to look you in the eye. 25 “And if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because he is My disciple, truly I tell you, he will never lose his reward.” — Matthew 10:42

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: We don't need tags to be claimed by grace.
excerpts.text: We live in a world that often ties worth to paperwork. To housing status. To appearance. To whether we have a place to return to. But grace doesn’t require credentials. Jesus never said, “Show Me your résumé.” He said: “Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.” — Matthew 11:28 27 All. No collar required. There was a man who spent months trying to get his ID back. He’d lost it in the shuffle — a shelter here, a hospital there, a bag stolen while he was asleep on the sidewalk. He told us, “It’s like the world forgot I exist.” But then someone called him by name. Someone said, “You’re on the list.” And he wept.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: We don't need tags to be claimed by grace.
excerpts.text: Not because of the paperwork. But because someone still saw him as someone worth counting. “I will not forget you. See, I have engraved you on the palms of My hands.” — Isaiah 49:15–16 Even when the world loses track of us, God does not. Even if we’re undocumented, displaced, or unnamed, He knows exactly where we are — and to Him, we are not missing. We are beloved. The little dog with no collar? She never left. She didn’t need an owner’s permission to be accepted. She just needed someone to care. And now, years later, she still sleeps on the welcome mat at the church door. Because someone made space. And someone welcomed her.

There’s something about steps that keep going even when they hurt. Something holy about a journey that no one else sees — but God does. One man said, “I used to think I had to clean myself up before I could be worth anything again. But now I think — maybe the fact that I’m still walking is the miracle.” Still walking. It is a miracle indeed. Sometimes we think healing has to be quick. That faith has to look strong. That only progress counts if it’s measurable.

29 But the Shepherd sees every step. Even the shaky ones. Even the ones that double back. Even the ones where we fall. “The steps of a good man are ordered by the Lord, and He delights in his way. Though he fall, he shall not be utterly cast down, for the Lord upholds him with His hand.” — Psalm 37:23–24 You might not be where you hoped you’d be by now. You might have taken more wrong turns than you can count. But if you’re still trying — still walking — God hasn’t given up on you.

The prodigal son didn’t run home. He limped. He had dust on his feet, shame on his shoulders, a speech in his mouth he never had to finish. Because even though he was still far away, his father had already run out to meet him. “While he was still a long way off…” — Luke 15:20 God doesn’t wait at the finish line. He meets us on the road. 30 That old dog eventually stopped walking. But not because he gave up. He stopped because someone opened their door. They said, “You’ve been looking for something. You don’t have to anymore. You’re home.”

And for the first time in a long time, he lay down for the night without needing to go anywhere tomorrow. Every step you’ve taken — yes, even the wrong ones — have brought you here. And He’s still walking with you. Every mile. Every moment. You are not lost. You are on your way. 31

We often talk about “finding God.” As if we’ve been out searching for Him. But the truth is — we were the ones behind the dumpster. And He was the one who came looking. “The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.” — Luke 19:10 A man at a shelter once said, “I didn’t walk into that church looking for anything. I just wanted to warm up. But now I know — it was God who brought me there. He found me long before I knew I was missing.”

32 Jesus told a story about a shepherd. One sheep had wandered away. Just one. But the shepherd didn’t say, “Ninety-nine is good enough.” He left the others and went after the one that was lost. “If a man has a hundred sheep and one of them gets lost, what will he do? Won’t he leave the ninety-nine others in the wilderness and go to search for the one that is lost until he finds it?” — Luke 15:4 Not if he finds it. Until. Because that’s who God is. He does not stop searching.

Maybe you didn’t come looking for Him today. Maybe you’ve spent years feeling like you’re beyond reach, beyond grace, beyond the kind of rescue songs are written about. But the Shepherd sees where you are. And He’s already walking your way. “I will answer them before they even call to me. While they are still talking about their needs, I will go ahead and answer their prayers!” — Isaiah 65:24 That dog behind the dumpster never asked to be seen. He didn’t have the courage. He didn’t even know how to ask. But someone saw him anyway. Someone leaned down, came down to where he was. And that’s how healing began. Not because he went looking. But because love found him first.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Real home is not a place — it's a Person.
excerpts.text: She found what she’d been looking for. Safety. Food. Consistency. Care. In other words — home. We often think of “home” as a roof and four walls. And it can be. But there’s another kind of home. The kind we carry. The kind we find when we are known, loved, and not sent away. That kind of home doesn’t depend on real estate. It depends on relationships. 34 “Surely Your goodness and mercy will follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.” — Psalm 23:6

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Real home is not a place — it's a Person.
excerpts.text: The house of the Lord is not a place you visit. It’s a place where you are invited to stay, forever. Not because you earned it. Not because you’ve fixed everything about yourself and your life. But because the Shepherd lives there — and He wants you with Him. A guests at a shelter once said, “I’ve lived under porches and overpasses. But the first time someone remembered my name two weeks in a row… that’s when I felt like I’d come home.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Real home is not a place — it's a Person.
excerpts.text: He smiled. Then added, “I think God might’ve been following me this whole time.” Jesus didn’t come to give us only shelter. He came to give us Himself. “Remain in me, and I will remain in you.” — John 15:4 Abide. Remain. Stay. Dwell. That’s home. 35 Maybe you’re still searching. Maybe your life has been filled with too many goodbyes, too many doors that didn’t open, too many nights alone. But the Shepherd never left. He’s been walking beside you, even when you didn’t know it. And today, He’s not just offering a place. He’s offering Himself.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Real home is not a place — it's a Person.
excerpts.text: We’ve told stories about good but hurting dogs. But they were never the main character. They were there to remind us of something greater — of Someone greater. Because if a dog can wait at the door for someone who might never return, how much more does God wait for us? If a stray can offer the last crust to a friend, how much more does the Shepherd give? If a nameless dog with no collar can be welcomed with a bowl of water, how much more are you seen? Known? Loved?

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Real home is not a place — it's a Person.
excerpts.text: “I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep.” — John 10:11 37 You don’t have to earn your way into His flock. You can’t. You don’t have to fix everything first. You can’t. You don’t even have to know where you’re going. Because He knows. And He’s already moving towards you. Actually, He’s right there beside you. If today you’re without a place, please know — you are not without a Shepherd. You are not forgotten. You are not alone. Not now. Not ever.

Why write about dogs? Because they seem to naturally do many of the things that we struggle with as humans. They wake up excited about ordinary days. They forgive quickly and completely. They’re loyal without keeping score. They trust deeply, rest without guilt, and love unconditionally. They don’t worry 5 about their image or compete for status. They just... live well. As we’ve watched these good dogs over the years, we’ve begun to see something beautiful: they reflect the character of our good God in ways that both surprise and encourage us. Not because dogs are perfect; they’re not. But because they embody qualities that God values and wants to develop in us. Their enthusiasm mirrors God’s joy over each new day He gives us. Their faithfulness reflects His unchanging love. Their ability to forgive and trust again shows us something about grace and being saved.

6 means to live well. These aren’t just cute dog stories (though we hope you find them engaging). They’re invitations to consider how the same God who designed dogs to be naturally loving and faithful wants to develop those same qualities in us. We believe that our good God, who loves us deeply and is with us always, often uses the most ordinary things to teach us extraordinary truths. Sometimes that’s a sunset or a friendship or a moment of unexpected grace. And sometimes it’s a dog who wags his tail at 6:47 AM, reminding us that each day is a gift worth celebrating.

So whether you’re dealing with the pressure of high school, figuring out friendships, worrying about the future, or just trying to understand what it means to follow Jesus in daily life, we invite you to pause and consider what these good dogs might teach us about our good God. After all, if the Creator of the universe can speak through a burning bush, He can certainly use a Golden Retriever to remind us about enthusiasm, or a rescue dog to teach us about trust, or a loyal mutt to show us what faithfulness looks like.

7 We’re honored to share these observations with you. Our prayer is that through these simple stories, you’ll catch a glimpse of the God who loves you unconditionally, who is always faithful, and who walks with you through every season of life. 8

Max reminds us that enthusiasm isn’t about having perfect circumstances. It’s about choosing to be excited about the possibilities each new day brings. When we wake up with genuine eagerness for what God might do in and through us today, everything changes. The apostle Paul understood this. Even while imprisoned, he wrote letters overflowing with joy and excitement about God’s work. He wasn’t faking it or trying to be positive for the sake of it. He was genuinely enthusiastic about being part of God’s story.

10 "This is the day the Lord has made; let us rejoice and be glad in it." — Psalm 118:24 David didn’t write this on his best day. He wrote it knowing that every day, including rainy days, boring days, and difficult days, comes from God’s hands. When we start each morning remembering that God has given us this specific day for a specific purpose, we can wake up with Max-level enthusiasm. Teenage years aren’t a waiting room for real life to begin. They’re not a dress rehearsal. God has work for us to do, people for us to love, and growth for us to experience right now, today. That’s worth getting excited about.

So tomorrow morning, before checking our phone or thinking about everything there is to do, try waking up like Max. Thank God for giving us another day. Ask Him to help us see the possibilities instead of just the problems. Choose enthusiasm over apathy. After all, if a dog can be that excited about the same kibble and the same backyard every single day, imagine how thrilled we can be about serving the God who creates new mercies every morning.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: How to Walk Straight When Everything's Interesting
excerpts.text: Maybe it’s the notification ping that pulls us into a social media rabbit hole right when we meant to start homework. Maybe it’s the friend drama that consumes our thoughts during the class we actually need to pay attention to. Maybe it’s the worry about college applications that keeps us from being present with the people right in front of us. The world is full of fire hydrants and rustling bushes, metaphorically speaking. There will always be something seemingly urgent demanding our immediate attention. But just like Rosie’s owner gently guides her back to the path, we need something to keep us focused on what actually matters.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: How to Walk Straight When Everything's Interesting
excerpts.text: “Let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith.” — Hebrews 12:1-2 13 The Christian life isn’t a leisurely stroll where we can investigate every interesting distraction. It’s a race with a specific course and a clear finish line. But notice what keeps us on track: fixing our eyes on Jesus. When we keep looking at Him, His character, His love, His purposes, the distractions lose their pull. The social media drama seems less important when we remember we’re called to love others well. The academic pressure feels less overwhelming when we remember our identity comes from being God’s children, not our GPA.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: How to Walk Straight When Everything's Interesting
excerpts.text: This doesn’t mean we ignore everything else or become boring people with no interests. It means we learn to distinguish between what deserves our attention and what’s just noise. It means developing the discipline to stay focused on our actual direction instead of chasing every shiny thing that crosses our path. Just like Rosie’s owner uses gentle tugs to redirect her, set up gentle reminders to redirect ourselves. A Bible verse as our phone wallpaper. Asking ourselves, “Is this helping me love God and others better?” before making decisions. Having a friend who can lovingly call us out when we’re chasing squirrels.

But Charlie, Scout, and Baxter show us a different model. They’re not friends because they’re identical. They’re friends because they’ve chosen to walk through life together despite their differences. When one of them wants to sniff around the pond while another wants to chase tennis balls, they figure it out. They accommodate one another without losing themselves. Real friendship isn’t about finding our clone. It’s about finding people who will walk alongside us, who will wait when we need to process something, who will celebrate our victories and stick around during our struggles. It’s about being that kind of friend to others, too.

"As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another.” — Proverbs 27:17 17 Notice that this verse doesn’t say "as iron finds identical iron." Iron sharpens iron through friction, through difference, through the process of two distinct pieces helping each other become better. Good friends challenge us, support us, and help us grow in ways we never could alone. Jesus modeled this perfectly. His closest friends were fishermen, tax collectors, political fanatics, and those who doubted. They disagreed about things. They had different personalities and backgrounds. But they walked together, learned together, and became better people because of their friendship.

In our friend group, we might be the Charlie (steady and calming), the Scout (energetic and driven), or the Baxter (the unique connector). That’s perfect. God uses different types of people to accomplish different things. The question isn’t whether we’re all the same; it’s whether we’re committed to walking together. We need to be deliberate about good friendship. Just like the dog pack actively looks for one another, we need to be proactive about building and maintaining friendships. That might mean texting first, suggesting hangouts, remembering what’s important to our friends, or simply showing up consistently.

18 It also requires grace. Our friends will sometimes be in different moods, have different needs, or want to “sniff around the pond” when we want to keep moving. Learning to allow one another to be themselves without losing ourselves is one of the most valuable skills we’ll ever develop. Look around. Who are the people God has placed in our lives to walk alongside? How can we be a better friend to them? And remember: the best friendships aren’t about being perfect together; they’re about choosing to stick together while we all become who God created us to be.

The funny thing is, Luna is never in danger of running out of treats. Her humans love her and provide for her daily. But she can’t seem to believe that tomorrow’s treats are as guaranteed as today’s. So she hoards, and hoards, and hoards. All while missing out on the simple joy of actually enjoying what she has right now. 20 Luna’s treat hoarding might seem silly, but it reveals something deep about human nature, too. We’re all prone to thinking there’s not enough to go around. So we’d better grab what we can and hold on tight.

For teenagers, this might show up in different ways. Hoarding compliments. Being afraid to encourage others because it might diminish our own worth. Hoarding opportunities, unwilling to help a classmate succeed because we think their success somehow threatens ours. Hoarding time, energy, or attention, convinced that if we give too much away, we’ll run out. But here’s what Luna misses and what we often miss too: hoarding doesn’t actually create security. It creates anxiety. Those treats hidden under the couch aren’t bringing Luna joy; they’re just making her constantly worry about protecting them. Meanwhile, she’s missing out on the pleasure of actually experiencing generosity, both giving and receiving.

21 “Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.” — Luke 6:38 This isn’t a magic formula for getting rich quick. It’s a spiritual truth about how God’s kingdom works. When we live with open hands instead of clenched fists, we discover that God’s supply never runs out. There’s always enough love, enough grace, enough joy to share.

Jesus demonstrated this perfectly with the feeding of the five thousand. The disciples saw a problem: not enough food for way too many people. But Jesus saw an opportunity: when we give what we have to God, He multiplies it beyond what we could imagine. This means we can be generous with our encouragement, our time, our talents, our friendship, and even with all the stuff we have. We can celebrate others’ successes without feeling threatened. We can share our lunch with someone who forgot theirs. We

22 can use our skills to help classmates instead of only focusing on our own advancement. Being generous isn’t just about giving money (though that’s part of it as we get older). It’s about living from a place of plenty rather than not enough. It’s about trusting that God will provide what we need, so we’re free to give what others need. Look for ways to be generous in small ways. Compliment someone genuinely. Share notes with a struggling classmate. Listen patiently when a friend needs to vent. Text someone an encouraging word. Hold the door. Share our snacks.

Notice what happens. Not just to others, but to us. We’ll probably find that giving actually creates more joy than hoarding ever could. We might discover that there’s always enough kindness to go around. We might even realize that the more we give away, the more we seem to have. Luna will probably keep hiding treats under the couch cushions. But we don’t have to live like there’s not enough good things coming. God’s supply never runs out. 23

Today, Pepper is a different dog. She still has some sensitive moments, but she’s learned that not every hand will hurt her, not every voice will yell at her, not every change means danger. She’s discovered that 24 being vulnerable enough to trust actually leads to better things than self-protection ever could. Pepper’s story hits close to home for many of us. Maybe we’ve been hurt by someone we trusted: a friend who betrayed a secret, a parent who let us down, a teacher who was unfair, a romantic relationship that ended badly. Maybe we’ve learned that putting ourselves out there can lead to pain, so it seems safer to keep our guard up.

Like Pepper, we can develop protective strategies that make perfect sense given our experiences. We might become suspicious of new people. We might hold back our true feelings to avoid vulnerability. We might assume the worst about others’ motives. We might try to control everything to prevent future hurt. The problem is, while these strategies protect us from potential pain, they also protect us from potential joy. When we’re so focused on avoiding hurt that we can’t trust anyone, we miss out on the deep connections God designed us to experience.

25 “Trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways submit to him, and he will make your paths straight.” — Proverbs 3:5-6 This doesn’t mean we should be naive or ignore red flags in relationships. God gave us wisdom and discernment for good reasons. But it does mean that our ultimate trust should be placed in Someone who will never let us down, never betray us, never use our vulnerability against us.

When we learn to trust God completely, it actually becomes easier to trust people appropriately. We can take healthy risks in relationships because our security doesn’t depend on other people being perfect. We can be vulnerable because we know that even if someone hurts us, God will still be there to help us heal and grow. Trusting God doesn’t guarantee that people will never disappoint us. But it does guarantee that disappointment isn’t the end of the story. Just like the shelter staff helped Pepper learn to trust again, God can heal our ability to connect with others even after we’ve been hurt.

26 This process takes time. Pepper didn’t transform overnight, and neither will we. There will be setbacks, moments when old fears resurface, times when we retreat back into self-protection mode. That’s normal and okay. Healing isn’t linear. But here’s what Pepper discovered and what we can discover too: the risk of trusting is worth it. The alternative, a life of constant suspicion and emotional isolation, isn’t really protection. It’s just a different kind of prison. Start small. Look for people who have proven themselves trustworthy in little things. Practice being vulnerable in safe relationships. Ask God to heal the places where we’ve been hurt and to help us figure out who deserves our trust.

Most importantly, remember that our ability to trust and love again isn’t just about our own healing; it’s about our capacity to be part of God’s work in the world. Just like Pepper now helps other nervous rescue dogs learn to trust, our healed heart can become a safe place for others who are learning to trust again too. 27

Maybe we’ve noticed this in ourselves or others. The straight-A student who can’t admit when they don’t understand something. The athlete who won’t try a new sport because they might not immediately excel. The person who’s so concerned with looking cool that they miss out on activities that might actually be fun. The social media presence that’s so carefully curated that it doesn’t reflect real life at all. When we’re constantly performing or protecting our image, we miss out on the very experiences that make life meaningful. We become like Princess: technically impressive but somehow less alive than we could be.

29 “Do nothing out of selfish ambition or vain conceit. Rather, in humility value others above yourselves, not looking to your own interests but each of you to the interests of others.” — Philippians 2:3-4 Humility isn’t about thinking less of ourselves; it’s about thinking of ourselves less. It’s about being secure enough in God’s love that we don’t need to constantly prove our worth to others. When we know we’re valued by the Creator of the universe, we’re free to focus on loving and serving others instead of managing our image.

Jesus is the perfect example of this. He was literally God in human form. If anyone had the right to act superior, it was Him. But instead, He “made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant” (Philippians 2:7). He washed feet, hung out with social outcasts, and ultimately died for people who didn’t deserve it. Humility actually makes life more enjoyable, not less. When we’re not constantly worried about looking foolish, we can try new things. When we’re not competing with everyone around us, we can genuinely

30 celebrate others’ successes. When we’re not protecting an image, we can have authentic relationships. This doesn’t mean we shouldn’t take care of ourselves or pursue excellence. Princess’s problem isn’t that she’s well-groomed; it’s that her self-focus has made her unable to connect with others or enjoy simple pleasures. Try something that might make us look less than perfect. Ask a question in class that might seem obvious. Try a new activity where we’ll be beginners. Compliment someone who might normally be competition. Help someone who can’t help us back.

Notice what happens when we stop worrying so much about how we appear to others. We might discover that the energy we were spending on image management can be redirected toward actually living. We might find that people are drawn to authenticity more than perfection. We might even realize that being humble is actually more fun than being important. After all, Murphy the scruffy mutt with his tennis ball looked a lot happier than Princess with all her dignity. Sometimes the best way to find joy is to stop taking yourself so seriously.

Buddy has been doing this for eight years now. Through Sarah’s awkward middle school phase, her 32 parents’ stressful financial year, her grandmother’s death, and countless ordinary days that weren’t particularly good or bad. He’s never once called in sick to his job of being loyal. He’s never threatened to find a more exciting family. He just shows up, day after day, offering the same steady presence and unconditional love. In a world that celebrates flashy achievements and dramatic gestures, Buddy represents something equally valuable but often overlooked: faithfulness. He reminds us that the most important things in life aren’t the spectacular moments that make great stories; they’re the consistent, daily choices to love and serve others.

This is particularly challenging for teenagers, who are constantly surrounded by highlight reels on social media, pressure to achieve big things quickly, and a culture that gets bored easily. It’s tempting to think that unless we’re doing something extraordinary, we’re not making a difference. But faithfulness is extraordinary, even when it looks ordinary. The friend who texts us back consistently. The family member who helps with chores without being asked. The student who treats the lunch lady with the same respect as the principal. These might

33 not be Instagram-worthy moments, but they’re the building blocks of a meaningful life. “His master replied, ‘Well done, good and faithful servant! You have been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things.’” — Matthew 25:21 Notice that the master doesn’t say, “Well done, good and spectacular servant!” or “Well done, good and impressive servant!” He commends faithfulness. In God’s economy, consistency matters more than flash, and reliability counts more than brilliance. This is incredibly encouraging news for those of us who feel ordinary. We don’t have to cure cancer or become famous to live a significant life. We just have to be faithful with what God has given us: our relationships, our responsibilities, and our opportunities to serve.

Faithfulness means showing up even when we don’t feel like it. It means keeping our word even when it’s inconvenient. It means loving people through their difficult seasons, not just their fun ones. It means doing our homework, honoring our parents, and 34 treating friends well even when no one is watching or applauding. It also means being faithful to God. Not just during exciting spiritual highs, but during ordinary weeks when faith feels more like discipline than emotion. Reading our Bible even when it feels dry. Praying even when heaven feels silent. Choosing integrity even when cutting corners would be easier.

Here’s what Buddy understands that we often forget: faithfulness creates trust, and trust creates the foundation for everything else that matters. Sarah’s family can count on Buddy because he’s proven himself reliable over time. Similarly, when we’re consistently faithful in small things, people learn they can count on us for bigger things. Practice faithfulness in our daily life. Text our friends back promptly. Do what we say we’ll do. Show up on time. Keep the private things friends share with you private. Help with family chores without being asked. Be the person others can count on.

We might not end up with any trophies or social media fame. But we’ll build something better: the kind of character that makes us truly valuable to the people who matter most. And we’ll discover that 35 there’s deep satisfaction in being someone others can depend on, just like Buddy. After all, the world has plenty of flash-in-the-pan excitement. What it really needs is more people willing to love steadily, serve consistently, and stay faithful through all seasons. That’s a calling worthy of any life.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Why Naps Aren't a Sign of Weakness
excerpts.text: If you’re a teenager, you've probably been told that rest is basically laziness in disguise. The message from school, social media, college prep culture, everywhere is that you should always be doing something productive. Taking a break means falling behind. Relaxing means you’re not serious about success. But Walter suggests a different approach. He reminds us that rest isn’t the opposite of productivity. Rest is what makes productivity possible. A tired dog is an ineffective dog. A tired teenager is an ineffective teenager.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Why Naps Aren't a Sign of Weakness
excerpts.text: The irony is that our culture’s obsession with constant activity often makes us less effective, not more. When we’re exhausted, we make poor decisions. When we’re stressed, we can’t think clearly. When we’re running on empty, we’re more likely to snap at people we care about. 38 “Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.”

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Why Naps Aren't a Sign of Weakness
excerpts.text: — Matthew 11:28-29 This invitation from Jesus isn’t just about spiritual rest (though it includes that). It’s about the deep soul- rest that comes from knowing we don’t have to earn God’s love through our achievements. We don’t have to prove our worth through constant activity. We can rest because our value is already established. Even God rested. After creating the universe, He took a Sabbath day, not because He was tired, but because rest is built into the rhythm of healthy life. If the Creator of everything took time to rest, maybe we should too.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Why Naps Aren't a Sign of Weakness
excerpts.text: Rest isn’t being selfish; it’s being a good caretaker. Taking care of our physical and emotional energy means we’ll have more to give to others. Getting enough sleep, taking breaks, saying no to some activities aren’t signs of weakness. They’re signs of wisdom. 39 This doesn’t mean we should sleep sixteen hours a day like Walter (though teenagers do need more sleep than adults). It means building healthy rhythms of work and rest into our lives. It means recognizing that downtime isn’t wasted time; it’s recovery time.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Why Naps Aren't a Sign of Weakness
excerpts.text: Prioritize sleep. Our brain literally needs it to function properly. Those all-nighters might seem necessary, but they usually end up not helping. Take real breaks. Put the phone down. Step away from homework. Sit outside. Let our mind wander. Practice saying no. We don’t have to participate in every activity or accept every invitation. It’s okay to have times when we do nothing in our schedule. Don’t feel guilty about rest. We’re not being lazy when we take care of ourselves. We’re being responsible.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: Why Naps Aren't a Sign of Weakness
excerpts.text: Find our own version of Walter’s sunny spots. Maybe it’s reading for pleasure, taking walks, listening to music, or just sitting quietly. Figure out what actually restores us, not just what distracts us. Walter doesn’t apologize for his naps because he knows they’re necessary. We don’t have to apologize 40 for taking care of ourselves either. In a world that makes us believe that being super tired is a great thing, choosing rest is actually a radical act of faith: trusting that our worth isn’t measured by our output.

But here’s the thing about Bear’s courage: it’s not careless. He doesn’t pick fights or look for trouble. 42 Most of the time, he’s perfectly content to let the mail carrier, delivery drivers, and friendly neighbors go about their business. He’s not afraid of everything, and he’s not aggressive toward everyone. His courage is purposeful: showing courage only when someone he loves needs protection. Bear’s example shows us what real courage looks like. It’s not the absence of fear; it’s acting rightly even when he has fear, when something important is at stake. It’s not about being aggressive or confrontational. It’s about standing up when standing up matters.

Speak up when someone is being bullied, even if it makes us unpopular. Say no to something you know is wrong, even when everyone else is saying yes. Be honest about our faith, even in environments where that’s not cool. Sometimes courage is big and obvious, like Bear’s protective stance. But often it’s smaller and quieter: having a difficult conversation with a friend, admitting when you’ve made a mistake, or choosing to do the right thing even when no one is watching.

43 “Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.” — Joshua 1:9 God spoke these words to Joshua when he was about to lead the Israelites into unknown territory. Joshua was probably terrified: he had big shoes to fill and huge challenges ahead. But God didn’t say, “Don’t be afraid because nothing bad will happen.” He said, “Don’t be afraid because I’ll be with you.”

That’s the secret to Bear’s courage and the secret to ours: we don’t have to be strong enough to handle everything alone. We just have to be willing to act on what’s right, trusting that God will provide what we need in the moment. Courage isn’t about being fearless. It’s about being faithful despite our fears. Bear was probably nervous when he confronted that stranger, but Emma’s safety mattered more than his comfort. Similarly, when we choose courage, we’re saying that something matters more than our own comfort or safety.

44 This might mean defending someone who’s being excluded or mocked, refusing to participate in gossip, even when it’s juicy, being honest about our struggles instead of pretending to be perfect, standing up for our convictions even when it costs us socially, asking for help when we’re in over our heads, apologizing when we’ve hurt someone. Courage often involves protecting something valuable: relationships, integrity, truth, justice, or other people’s wellbeing. Like Bear, we need to figure out when courage is called for and when it’s not. Not every disagreement requires a confrontation. Not every wrong needs to be our personal crusade. Wisdom helps us pick our battles.

But when courage is called for, remember that we’re not alone. The same God who was with Joshua in the wilderness is with us in our high school hallways, family conversations, and friend groups. We can take the next right step, even when we’re scared, because He’s taking it with us. Start small. Look for small opportunities to practice courage. Speak up in class. Try something new. Have an honest conversation. Each time we choose courage over comfort, we’re building spiritual muscle for more difficult times.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: What to Do When You've Eaten the Couch
excerpts.text: Murphy learned from his mistake, but he didn’t let shame make him feel like he was a bad dog. Murphy’s story perfectly captures something we all struggle with: what to do when we mess up badly. And if you’re a teenager, mistakes feel extra painful because everything seems really important. A bad grade? Future ruined. A friendship conflict feels like the end of the world. A poor choice feels like it defines us forever. But Murphy shows us a healthier way to handle failure. He didn’t minimize what he did wrong. His guilt was real and appropriate. But he also didn’t let that guilt paralyze him or convince him that he was too bad to change. He faced what happened, learned from the experience, and moved forward.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: What to Do When You've Eaten the Couch
excerpts.text: The key difference between guilt and shame is this: guilt says, “I did something bad,” while shame says, “I 48 am bad.” Guilt can lead to positive change. Shame just leads to hiding and hopelessness. “If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins and purify us from all unrighteousness.” — 1 John 1:9 This verse contains one of the most liberating truths in all of Scripture: forgiveness is available, complete, and immediate for anyone who genuinely asks for it. We don’t have to earn it, work for it, or prove we deserve it. We just have to honestly admit what we did wrong and receive what God freely offers.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: What to Do When You've Eaten the Couch
excerpts.text: But forgiveness doesn’t stop with God. Jesus taught us to pray, “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors” (Matthew 6:12). Part of receiving forgiveness is learning to forgive others and to forgive ourselves. This is particularly challenging for teenagers, who often struggle with perfectionism and self-criticism. We might find it easier to forgive friends for their mistakes than to forgive ourselves for ours. But 49 holding onto guilt after God has forgiven us isn’t humility; it’s pride. It’s as if we are saying that our standards are higher than God’s.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: What to Do When You've Eaten the Couch
excerpts.text: Admit the damage honestly. Don’t minimize, excuse, or blame others. Take responsibility for our choices and their consequences. Make things right where possible. Apologize sincerely. Replace what can be replaced. Learn what needs to be learned. Accept forgiveness gracefully. Whether it comes from God, others, or ourselves, receive it as the gift it is. Don’t keep punishing ourselves after we’ve already made it right. Learn and move forward. Let the experience teach you something valuable, but don’t let it define you. Extend the same grace to others. When friends mess up, remember how good it felt to be forgiven instead of criticized.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: What to Do When You've Eaten the Couch
excerpts.text: Murphy didn’t stay on the couch that he partially destroyed feeling sorry for himself. He got back to the business of being a good dog, with better understanding for how to handle his anxiety. Like Murphy, our mistakes don’t disqualify us from our purpose; they can actually equip us to help others who are struggling with similar issues. 50 Everyone messes up. Everyone needs forgiveness. Everyone deserves a chance to start over. The question isn’t whether we’ll make mistakes; it’s whether we’ll let those mistakes teach us about the grace of God and the chance to start again.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: What to Do When You've Eaten the Couch
excerpts.text: Remember: we are not the worst thing we’ve ever done. We are beloved children of God. We are learning how to live wisely, and sometimes that learning involves some pretty spectacular failures. That’s not a bug in the system; it’s a feature. Even couch-eating mistakes can become part of our testimony about God’s grace. 51

Daisy shows us something beautiful about true service: it’s not about us. It’s not about feeling good about ourselves, getting praise, or even seeing results right away. It’s about showing up every time and offering whatever others need, whether that’s energy, calm, entertainment, or simply being there. For teenagers, this can be an unusual idea. We’re often encouraged to serve in ways that look good on college applications or make us feel important. But Daisy reminds us that the most meaningful service often happens quietly, without fanfare, in response to real needs around us.

Real service requires some of Daisy’s key qualities: patience (things don’t always go as planned), flexibility (different people need different things), and 53 persistence (showing up even when you don’t feel like it). It also requires genuine care for others rather than doing it for ourselves. “Each of you should use whatever gift you have to serve others, as faithful stewards of God’s grace in its various forms.” — 1 Peter 4:10 We have gifts to offer, not someday when we’re older and more qualified, but right now. Serving others and being a good steward is connected. The abilities, opportunities, and resources we have aren’t just for our own benefit. They’re tools God has blessed us with so that we can also bless others.

Jesus is a perfect example. Even though He was literally God, He spent His time washing dirty feet, healing sick people, feeding hungry crowds, and listening to individuals who needed help. In Mark 10:43, He said: “Whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant.” This doesn’t mean we have to become a therapy dog. It means looking for ways to serve that match our gifts 54 and the needs around us. Good with kids? Help with Sunday school or babysit for free for a struggling family. Tech savvy? Teach older adults how to use smartphones or help teachers with computer problems. A good listener? Simply be available when friends need to talk through problems. Organized? Help coordinate service projects or assist with community events. Athletic? Coach younger kids or organize activities for people with disabilities.

Start where we are, with what we have, serving people we can actually reach. Daisy doesn’t try to help every sick child in the world; she faithfully serves the ones in her hospital. We don’t have to solve global problems to make a real difference. Also, like Daisy, be prepared for service to sometimes be harder than expected. People might not appreciate our efforts. Our help might not solve their problems completely or even a little bit. We might not see results right away, and that’s okay. Faithful service is about unchanging love.

Look for ways to serve that’s not about us at all. Don’t post about it on social media. Don’t expect thank-you notes. Don’t count it toward service hours. Just quietly help someone who needs it, the way Daisy helps her young patients. 55 We might discover that serving others is actually one of the most fulfilling things we can do. Not because it makes us look good, but because it connects us to our purpose and lets us be part of God’s work of healing the world, one small act of kindness at a time.

57 won’t.” He doesn’t pace anxiously or give up after ten minutes. He doesn’t need to be reminded all the time that the bus really is on its way. He just waits with calm certainty, because in his experience, Katie always comes home. Max’s window waiting is a perfect picture of hope. Not the wishful-thinking kind of hope (like hoping it won’t rain on our outdoor party), but the kind of hope that trusts without doubting. Max waits because he knows Katie’s character, he knows her promises, and he’s experienced her faithfulness day after day.

Teenagers require a lot of hope. Hope that hard work in school will pay off. Hope that the friendships will last. Hope that dreams are worth the effort. Hope that the world can be better. But sometimes hope feels risky. What if we trust someone and they let us down? What if we work toward something and it doesn’t happen? What if we hope for good things, but life doesn’t go the way we wanted? It can seem safer to expect less, hope for less, trust less.

58 “And hope does not put us to shame, because God’s love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, who has been given to us.” — Romans 5:5 This verse promises that hope in God never leads to disappointment. Not because everything always goes the way we want, but because God’s love is the one thing that never fails. Katie might one day not go to school for one reason or another, but God’s character never changes. His promises never expire. His love never runs out.

This means we can wait like Max does with trust in God’s goodness, even when we can’t see it yet. We can trust Him to guide us when we make decisions, His strength in our struggles, His peace in our anxiety, and His purpose in our lives. Not because we’re optimistic people, but because we know His track record. Biblical hope isn’t naive or passive. It’s active trust based on God’s proven faithfulness. It says, “I don't know exactly how this will work out, but I know who’s in control, and I trust His heart toward me.”

59 This kind of hope changes how we live right now. We can work hard in school without being anxious about the future, because we trust God with the outcomes. We can invest in relationships without fear of being rejected, because our security comes from God’s unchanging love. We can take healthy risks and try new things, because we know that even failures can be part of God’s good plan for our growth. We can face difficult times with courage, because we trust that God can bring good from even painful situations.

Hope also changes how we treat others. When we’re secure in God’s love and trust His plans for us, we can give a lot of encouragement, be patient with difficult people, and believe others can grow and change. Let’s build this kind of hope. Keep track of God’s faithfulness in our lives. When we pray about something and see an answer, write it down. When God provides what we need, make a note. When He gives us strength for a challenge or peace during hard times, make sure to put it in writing. Over time, we will have our own collection of proof that God keeps His promises.

Max waits by the window every day because Katie has never failed to come home. As we experience God’s faithfulness in small things, we will find it easier to 60 trust Him with bigger things. Our hope will feel less like a maybe and more like something we can count on. Take our position by the window of faith. Wait with confidence for God’s goodness. And remember: the same God who has been faithful to a lot of people for thousands of years is the same God who loves us and has good plans for our lives today. That’s worth waiting for with joy.

61 Final Thoughts If you’ve made it this far, you’ve probably noticed something: dogs don’t read theology books or attend seminars on spiritual growth. They don’t have complicated ways for living well. They just know to do many of the things that make life meaningful. They’re excited, focused, loyal, generous, trusting, humble, faithful, rested, courageous, forgiving, serving, and hopeful. But here’s what makes their example even more powerful: dogs live this way naturally because they know they’re loved and cared for by their families. Because they feel safe in that relationship they can be the best dogs they can be.

That’s exactly how God designed the Christian life to work. When we truly realize how much God loves us, not because we’ve earned it, but because of who He is, we’re free to live with the same kind of joy and trust that we see in good dogs. You don’t have to wait until you’re older, more mature, or have everything figured out to start living this way. You can wake up tomorrow with Max’s 62 excitement, walk through your day with Buddy’s faithfulness, and rest each night with Walter’s peace.

The world needs more people who live like good dogs: people who love without any conditions, serve without counting the cost, and trust in something bigger than themselves. People who can be disappointed without growing bitter, who can fail without losing hope, who can succeed without losing humility. As you go through the ups and downs of these teenage years, remember that you’re not just figuring out what you want to do with your life. You’re discovering who God created you to be. And the same One who made dogs so naturally good at loving and living has even better plans, much better plans, for you.

So wag your tail at the gift of each new day. Stay close to the people who matter. Love generously. Trust deeply. Rest when you need to. Be brave when courage is called for. Forgive quickly. Serve joyfully. Hope confidently. In other words, live like you know you're loved. Because you are. The good dogs have been trying to tell us this all along. Maybe it’s time we started listening. 63

5 or our family’s trust? Dealing with addiction that has destroyed relationships? Betrayed someone who loved us? Hurt our children through our poor decisions? Feel torn apart…? Like being ripped from the life we once knew, like we have too much baggage, like our mistakes have made us unlovable. We want you to know something before you read these twelve lessons: You are still loved. Not because our mistakes don’t matter. They do. Not because consequences aren’t real. They are. Not because we don’t have work to do. We do.

But because being torn doesn’t make us worthless. Because people can make terrible choices and still be people who need love, forgiveness, and second chances. Because love doesn’t depend on having a perfect, untorn life. The dogs in this book taught us that being in an impossibly difficult place doesn’t erase our value. It just means we are in need of care, patience, and knowing that Someone does see our heart instead of just our history. We believe God sees us that way.

6 These are not complicated theology lessons. These are simple stories about hope finding dogs in challenging situations. Love founding their torn-up lives. People who wanted them exactly as they were and taking them home. If that sounds like something you need to hear, this book is for you. We are all torn in some way. But we are still loved by God. And that makes all the difference. 7 A Note About These Stories The dogs and people in this book are based on real dogs and real people. We have observed these behaviors, situations, and outcomes at shelters and in life. However, all names have been changed to protect privacy. Some details have been combined or adjusted to better illustrate the lessons, but every story is grounded in actual experiences we have witnessed.

excerpts.lesson_title: Good Dogs Don't Stop Being Good
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On Keeping Your Character
excerpts.text: 10 “This dog isn’t aggressive,” she tells the Matthews family. “He had a resource-guarding response to an unexpected situation. With proper management and some retraining, he can absolutely remain a safe family pet.” “Everyone makes mistakes,” she adds. “Even good dogs. That doesn’t erase who they really are.” We’ve all had our Kingsman moment. That one terrible decision, that moment when fear or anger or weakness took over and we did something that horrified even ourselves. Maybe we lost our temper and said unforgivable words. Maybe we made a choice that destroyed a marriage. Maybe we betrayed someone’s trust or hurt someone we love.

excerpts.lesson_title: Good Dogs Don't Stop Being Good
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On Keeping Your Character
excerpts.text: Now we carry our own warning label. Every time we meet someone new, we wonder if they know. Every time we look in the mirror, we see that red flag. We’ve convinced ourselves that one terrible moment defines our entire worth. But God doesn’t see us that way. 11 “The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.” — 1 Samuel 16:7 God spoke these words when everyone was judging by external appearances, by past performance, by what seemed obvious on the surface. But God sees deeper. He sees our heart, not just the moment when it failed, but what it’s truly made of.

excerpts.lesson_title: Good Dogs Don't Stop Being Good
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On Keeping Your Character
excerpts.text: We live in a world that loves to label people by their worst moment. One mistake becomes our permanent identity. One failure defines our future. One bad decision erases years of good character. But God’s perspective is different. He sees Kingsman as a good dog who had a scared moment, not a dangerous dog who occasionally acts nice. He sees us as His beloved children who stumbled, not as failures who occasionally do something right. Kingsman’s worth wasn’t determined by that one afternoon in the yard. Three years of gentleness, patience, and love couldn’t be erased by one moment

excerpts.lesson_title: Good Dogs Don't Stop Being Good
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On Keeping Your Character
excerpts.text: 12 of fear. The behaviorist helped him see his true character. And that’s exactly how God sees us. Our identity isn’t our worst decision. Our worth isn’t our biggest failure. Our future isn’t imprisoned by our past. We are who God says we are: loved, valuable, worthy of second chances. The warning label feels permanent, but it’s not. Reputations can be rebuilt. Hearts can heal. Good dogs who make mistakes are still good dogs. They just need proper support and people who see their heart.

excerpts.lesson_title: Good Dogs Don't Stop Being Good
excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On Keeping Your Character
excerpts.text: God is like that experienced behaviorist. He sees our whole history, not just our worst moment. He looks at our heart and says, “That’s my child who had a bad moment. That doesn’t erase who they really are.” Our warning label doesn’t have to be permanent. We’re still worthy of love. We’re still valuable to God. 13

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On God's Unchanging Faithfulness
excerpts.text: When we’re dealing with the aftermath of our big mistakes, it feels like everything reliable in our life 15 disappears. Friends distance themselves. Family members lose trust. Opportunities vanish. The future we planned crumbles. We feel abandoned by everyone who once mattered to us. But in the middle of all this loss and change, God’s faithfulness remains absolutely constant. His love doesn’t depend on our performance. His provision doesn’t fluctuate with our failures. His care for us isn’t conditional on our track record.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On God's Unchanging Faithfulness
excerpts.text: “Because of the Lord’s great love, we are not consumed, for His compassion never fails. They are new every morning; great is Your faithfulness.” — Lamentations 3:22-23 The prophet Jeremiah wrote these words during the darkest period of his life. His nation was destroyed, his people were in exile, and everything he had worked for was in ruins. He had every reason to believe God had abandoned him. But in the middle of his despair, Jeremiah remembered this truth: God’s compassion never fails.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On God's Unchanging Faithfulness
excerpts.text: 16 Never. Every morning brings fresh mercy, renewed grace, constant faithfulness. Notice that God’s faithfulness isn’t dependent on our circumstances improving. Jeremiah was still in exile when he wrote these words. His situation hadn’t changed. But what did change was his perspective on God’s unchanging character. Rico’s daily bread wasn’t dependent on his adoptability, his behavior, or his popularity with visitors. It came because the shelter had committed to caring for him, period. The feeding schedule didn’t change based on Rico’s performance or prospects.

excerpts.lesson_subtitle: On God's Unchanging Faithfulness
excerpts.text: God’s daily provision works the same way. His love for us isn’t performance-based. His care isn’t conditional on our success in rebuilding our lives. His faithfulness doesn’t waver when we have setbacks or when healing takes longer than expected. The people in our lives may come and go. Some relationships may never recover from our mistakes. But God’s commitment to us is like that 7:15 a.m. feeding schedule: absolutely reliable, completely consistent, never dependent on external circumstances. 17 When relationships feel unstable and your future feels uncertain, remind yourself: the feeding schedule hasn’t changed. God’s commitment to caring for you is not dependent on other people’s commitment to forgiving you. God’s faithfulness can be your constant, too. People may leave, but His love keeps showing up at 7:15 every morning.

We all have a kennel card that tells our story. Divorced. Fired. Bankrupted. Relapsed. Failed. The list of our returns and mistakes feels like a permanent record that disqualifies us from being chosen by anyone worthwhile. When people get to know us, we brace for the moment they discover our history. We watch their faces change when they learn about our bankruptcy, our addiction, our infidelity, our arrest record. We’ve been returned before, and we expect it to happen again.

But God doesn’t choose people based on clean records. 21 “But God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise; God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong. God chose the lowly things of this world and the despised things, and the things that are not, to nullify the things that are.” — 1 Corinthians 1:27-28 Paul wrote this to people who felt unqualified for God’s love. They looked around at successful, respectable, religious people and assumed God preferred that type. They read their own kennel cards and assumed they were damaged goods.

But Paul explains that God’s selection process is backwards from human logic. He doesn’t choose people in spite of their failures - He often chooses them because of their failures. Broken people understand grace in ways that successful people never do. The word “chose” here is active and intentional. God didn’t settle for us because the good people weren’t available. He specifically selected us, knowing our full history, because He saw something He wanted. 22 The Martinez family didn’t choose Luna despite her past. They chose her because they understood what her past meant. Her escape attempts weren’t rebellion; they were evidence of how deeply she loves and how desperately she fears abandonment. Her “failures” revealed her heart.

God reads our whole story the same way. Our mistakes don’t disqualify us from His love. They qualify us to understand His grace. Our failures don’t make us undesirable. They make us precisely the kind of people He’s looking for. When God looks at our kennel card, He doesn’t see damaged goods. He sees someone who will understand mercy because they’ve needed it. He sees someone who will love deeply because they know what it costs. He sees someone whose story of redemption will encourage others who feel unredeemable.

When you’re tempted to settle for relationships with people who will accept you “despite” your past, hold out for people who love you “because of” your whole story, including the broken parts. Someone is looking for you. Someone who will read your whole story and say, “That’s exactly the person I want.” 23

When our life falls apart, when we lose our job, our marriage, our reputation, everything feels scarce. Money, opportunities, support, hope. It all seems less now. We’re living with smaller portions of everything we once took for granted. In this scarcity, we face Tank’s choice. Do we hoard what little we have left? Do we pull back from helping others because we don’t have enough for ourselves? Do we use our crisis as an excuse to become selfish? Or do we become more generous, not less?

“All they asked was that we should continue to remember the poor, the very thing I had been eager to do.” — Galatians 2:10 26 Paul wrote this while describing a meeting with church leaders about his ministry. Even though Paul was facing persecution, financial hardship, and constant danger, the one thing the leaders emphasized was continuing to help others who had even less. Notice they didn’t say “help the poor when your life is stable” or “be generous when you have plenty.” They said continue to remember the poor even when you’re struggling, even when your own resources are limited, even when it would be understandable to focus only on your own survival.

True generosity isn’t giving when we have excess. It’s sharing when we have little. Tank’s generosity meant more during the rationing than it would have during abundance. When food was plentiful, sharing was easy. When it was limited, sharing was costly. And therefore more meaningful. Our generosity means more now, in our season of loss and limitation, than it did when we had plenty. When people see us sharing our limited resources, caring for others despite our own struggles, choosing to help even when we need help ourselves… That reveals character that abundance never could.

27 The person who gives generously when they’re wealthy might just have good financial planning. The person who shares their last twenty dollars with someone hungrier than they are? That’s someone whose heart has been changed by understanding what it means to receive mercy. Let others go first: in conversations, in opportunities, in receiving help or attention. Use your strength, whatever form it takes, to create space for those who are smaller or weaker. If or when abundance returns to your life, remember the lessons you learned during rationing. Character built in scarcity doesn’t have to disappear in prosperity. Let your current difficulties reveal the best parts of who you are, not the worst.

Later, the shelter manager tells the story to anyone who’ll listen. “Diesel saved lives today. He found his voice again when it really mattered.” After the incident that ended his police career, Diesel learned that his judgment could be wrong, that his voice could cause problems, that maybe it was safer to 31 let others make the decisions. Staying quiet meant avoiding the risk of being wrong again. Even when he saw the flooding, Diesel was tempted to wait for someone else to notice, someone else to take responsibility, someone else to risk being wrong. It would have been easier to stay silent and let others decide whether this was worth responding to. But silence would have cost lives.

When we make a big mistake that damages our credibility, when we’re wrong about something important, when our judgment fails us, when our voice leads to harm, we often overcorrect by going completely silent.
